**Croatia**,[[d]](#cite_note-12) officially the **Republic of Croatia**,[[e]](#cite_note-13) is a country in [Central](/wiki/Central_Europe "Central Europe") and [Southeast Europe](/wiki/Southeast_Europe "Southeast Europe"), on the coast of the [Adriatic Sea](/wiki/Adriatic_Sea "Adriatic Sea"). It borders [Slovenia](/wiki/Slovenia "Slovenia") to the northwest, [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary "Hungary") to the northeast, [Serbia](/wiki/Serbia "Serbia") to the east, [Bosnia and Herzegovina](/wiki/Bosnia_and_Herzegovina "Bosnia and Herzegovina") and [Montenegro](/wiki/Montenegro "Montenegro") to the southeast, and shares a [maritime border](/wiki/Maritime_border "Maritime border") with [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy") to the west. Its capital and largest city, [Zagreb](/wiki/Zagreb "Zagreb"), forms one of the country's [primary subdivisions](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_Croatia "Administrative divisions of Croatia"), with [twenty counties](/wiki/Counties_of_Croatia "Counties of Croatia"). Other major urban centers include [Split](/wiki/Split,_Croatia "Split, Croatia"), [Rijeka](/wiki/Rijeka "Rijeka") and [Osijek](/wiki/Osijek "Osijek"). The country spans 56,594 square kilometres (21,851 square miles), and has a population of nearly 3.9 million.

The [Croats](/wiki/Croats "Croats") arrived in modern-day Croatia, then part of [Roman Illyria](/wiki/Illyria "Illyria"), in the late 6th century. In the 7th century, they organized the territory into [two duchies](/wiki/Duchy_of_Croatia "Duchy of Croatia"). Croatia was first internationally recognized as independent on 7 June 879 during the reign of Duke [Branimir](/wiki/Branimir_of_Croatia "Branimir of Croatia"). [Tomislav](/wiki/Tomislav_of_Croatia "Tomislav of Croatia") became the first king by 925, elevating Croatia to the status of [a kingdom](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Croatia_(925%E2%80%931102) "Kingdom of Croatia (925–1102)"). During the succession crisis after the [Trpimirović dynasty](/wiki/Trpimirovi%C4%87_dynasty "Trpimirović dynasty") ended, [Croatia entered](/wiki/Croatia_in_personal_union_with_Hungary "Croatia in personal union with Hungary") a [personal union](/wiki/Personal_union "Personal union") with [Hungary](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Hungary_(1000%E2%80%931301) "Kingdom of Hungary (1000–1301)") in 1102. In 1527, faced with [Ottoman conquest](/wiki/Hundred_Years%27_Croatian%E2%80%93Ottoman_War "Hundred Years' Croatian–Ottoman War"), the [Croatian Parliament](/wiki/Croatian_Parliament "Croatian Parliament") elected [Ferdinand I of Austria](/wiki/Ferdinand_I,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Ferdinand I, Holy Roman Emperor") to the Croatian throne. In October 1918, the [State of Slovenes, Croats, and Serbs](/wiki/State_of_Slovenes,_Croats_and_Serbs "State of Slovenes, Croats and Serbs"), independent from the [Habsburg Empire](/wiki/Habsburg_monarchy "Habsburg monarchy"), was proclaimed in Zagreb, and in December 1918, it [merged](/wiki/Creation_of_Yugoslavia "Creation of Yugoslavia") into the [Kingdom of Yugoslavia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Yugoslavia "Kingdom of Yugoslavia"). Following the [Axis](/wiki/Axis_powers "Axis powers") [invasion of Yugoslavia](/wiki/Invasion_of_Yugoslavia "Invasion of Yugoslavia") in April 1941, most of Croatia was incorporated into a Nazi-installed [puppet state](/wiki/List_of_World_War_II_puppet_states "List of World War II puppet states"), the [Independent State of Croatia](/wiki/Independent_State_of_Croatia "Independent State of Croatia"). A [resistance movement](/wiki/Croatian_Partisans "Croatian Partisans") led to the creation of the [Socialist Republic of Croatia](/wiki/Socialist_Republic_of_Croatia "Socialist Republic of Croatia"), which after the war became a founding member and constituent of the [Socialist Federal Republic of Yugoslavia](/wiki/Socialist_Federal_Republic_of_Yugoslavia "Socialist Federal Republic of Yugoslavia"). On 25 June 1991, Croatia [declared its independence from Yugoslavia](/wiki/Independence_of_Croatia "Independence of Croatia"), and the [War of Independence](/wiki/Croatian_War_of_Independence "Croatian War of Independence") was successfully fought over the next four years.

Croatia is a [republic](/wiki/Republic "Republic") and a [parliamentary democracy](/wiki/Parliamentary_democracy "Parliamentary democracy"). It is a member of the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"), the [Eurozone](/wiki/Eurozone "Eurozone"), the [Schengen Area](/wiki/Schengen_Area "Schengen Area"), [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO"), the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations"), the [Council of Europe](/wiki/Council_of_Europe "Council of Europe"), the [OSCE](/wiki/Organization_for_Security_and_Co-operation_in_Europe "Organization for Security and Co-operation in Europe"), the [World Trade Organization](/wiki/World_Trade_Organization "World Trade Organization"), a founding member of the [Union for the Mediterranean](/wiki/Union_for_the_Mediterranean "Union for the Mediterranean"), and is currently in the process of joining the [OECD](/wiki/OECD "OECD"). An active participant in [United Nations peacekeeping](/wiki/United_Nations_peacekeeping "United Nations peacekeeping"), Croatia contributed troops to the [International Security Assistance Force](/wiki/International_Security_Assistance_Force "International Security Assistance Force") and was elected to fill a non-permanent seat on the [United Nations Security Council](/wiki/United_Nations_Security_Council "United Nations Security Council") in the [2008–2009 term](/wiki/2007_United_Nations_Security_Council_election "2007 United Nations Security Council election") for the first time.

Croatia is a [developed country](/wiki/Developed_country "Developed country") with an advanced [high-income economy](/wiki/World_Bank_high-income_economy "World Bank high-income economy").[[9]](#cite_note-14) [Service](/wiki/Service_(economics) "Service (economics)"), [industrial sectors](/wiki/Secondary_sector_of_the_economy "Secondary sector of the economy"), and agriculture dominate the [economy](/wiki/Economy_of_Croatia "Economy of Croatia"). [Tourism](/wiki/Tourism_in_Croatia "Tourism in Croatia") is a significant source of revenue for the country, with nearly 20 million tourist arrivals as of 2019.[[10]](#cite_note-15)[[11]](#cite_note-16)[[12]](#cite_note-17) Since the 2000s, the [Croatian government](/wiki/Government_of_Croatia "Government of Croatia") has heavily invested in infrastructure, especially [transport routes and facilities](/wiki/Transport_in_Croatia "Transport in Croatia") along the [Pan-European corridors](/wiki/Pan-European_corridors "Pan-European corridors"). Croatia has also positioned itself as a regional energy leader in the early 2020s and is contributing to the diversification of Europe's energy supply via its floating [liquefied natural gas](/wiki/Liquefied_natural_gas "Liquefied natural gas") import terminal off [Krk](/wiki/Krk "Krk") island, [LNG Hrvatska](/wiki/LNG_Hrvatska "LNG Hrvatska").[[13]](#cite_note-lng-18) Croatia provides [social security](/wiki/Welfare_spending "Welfare spending"), [universal health care](/wiki/Healthcare_in_Croatia "Healthcare in Croatia"), and tuition fee-free [primary and secondary education](/wiki/Education_in_Croatia "Education in Croatia") while supporting [culture](/wiki/Culture_of_Croatia "Culture of Croatia") through public institutions and corporate investments in [media](/wiki/Mass_media_in_Croatia "Mass media in Croatia") and [publishing](/wiki/Croatian_literature "Croatian literature").

## Etymology

Main article: [Names of the Croats and Croatia](/wiki/Names_of_the_Croats_and_Croatia "Names of the Croats and Croatia")

Croatia's [non-native](/wiki/Endonym_and_exonym "Endonym and exonym") [name](/wiki/Names_of_the_Croats_and_Croatia "Names of the Croats and Croatia") derives from [Medieval Latin](/wiki/Medieval_Latin "Medieval Latin") *Croātia*, itself a derivation of [North-West Slavic](/wiki/West_Slavic_languages "West Slavic languages") \**Xərwate*, by [liquid metathesis](/wiki/Slavic_liquid_metathesis_and_pleophony "Slavic liquid metathesis and pleophony") from Common Slavic period *\*Xorvat*, from proposed [Proto-Slavic](/wiki/Proto-Slavic_language "Proto-Slavic language") *[\*Xъrvátъ](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/Reconstruction:Proto-Slavic/x%D1%8Arvat%D1%8A "wikt:Reconstruction:Proto-Slavic/xъrvatъ")* which possibly comes from the 3rd-century [Scytho-Sarmatian](/wiki/Scythian_languages "Scythian languages") form attested in the [Tanais Tablets](/wiki/Tanais_Tablets "Tanais Tablets") as Χοροάθος (**Khoroáthos**, alternate forms comprise *Khoróatos* and **Khoroúathos**).[[14]](#cite_note-Gluhak-1993-19) The origin of the ethnonym is uncertain, but most probably is from [Proto-Ossetian](/wiki/Ossetian_language "Ossetian language") / [Alanian](/wiki/Scythian_languages#Alanian "Scythian languages") \**xurvæt-* or \**xurvāt-*, in the meaning of "one who guards" ("guardian, protector").[[15]](#cite_note-20)

The oldest preserved record of the Croatian ethnonym's native variation *\*xъrvatъ* is of the variable stem, attested in the [Baška tablet](/wiki/Ba%C5%A1ka_tablet "Baška tablet") in [style](/wiki/Style_(form_of_address) "Style (form of address)") zvъnъmirъ kralъ xrъvatъskъ ("[Zvonimir](/wiki/Demetrius_Zvonimir_of_Croatia "Demetrius Zvonimir of Croatia"), [Croatian king](/wiki/List_of_rulers_of_Croatia "List of rulers of Croatia")"),[[16]](#cite_note-Fučić-1971-21) while the Latin variation *Croatorum* is archaeologically confirmed on a church inscription found in [Bijaći](/wiki/Bija%C4%87i "Bijaći") near [Trogir](/wiki/Trogir "Trogir") dated to the end of the 8th or early 9th century.[[17]](#cite_note-22) The presumably oldest stone [inscription](/wiki/Epigraphy "Epigraphy") with fully preserved ethnonym is the 9th-century [Branimir inscription](/wiki/Branimir_inscription "Branimir inscription") found near [Benkovac](/wiki/Benkovac "Benkovac"), where Duke [Branimir](/wiki/Branimir_of_Croatia "Branimir of Croatia") is styled *Dux Cruatorvm*, likely dated between 879 and 892, during his rule.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMužić2007195–198-23) The Latin term **Chroatorum** is attributed to a charter of Duke [Trpimir I of Croatia](/wiki/Trpimir_I_of_Croatia "Trpimir I of Croatia"), dated to 852 in a 1568 copy of a lost original, but it is not certain if the original was indeed older than the Branimir inscription.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMužić200727-24)[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMužić2007171-25)

## History

Main article: [History of Croatia](/wiki/History_of_Croatia "History of Croatia")

### Prehistory and antiquity

Main article: [History of Croatia before the Croats](/wiki/History_of_Croatia_before_the_Croats "History of Croatia before the Croats")

[![Ceramic sculpture](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/ZGvucdove.jpg/120px-ZGvucdove.jpg)](/wiki/File:ZGvucdove.jpg)

[![Stone Sculpture](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/Croatian_Apoxyomenos_Louvre_n04.jpg/120px-Croatian_Apoxyomenos_Louvre_n04.jpg)](/wiki/File:Croatian_Apoxyomenos_Louvre_n04.jpg)

Left: The [Vučedol dove](/wiki/Vu%C4%8Dedol_dove "Vučedol dove"), a sculpture from 2800 to 2500 BC.  
Right: *[Croatian Apoxyomenos](/wiki/Croatian_Apoxyomenos "Croatian Apoxyomenos")*, [Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece") statue, 2nd or 1st century BC.

The area known as Croatia today was inhabited throughout the [prehistoric period](/wiki/Prehistoric_period "Prehistoric period"). [Neanderthal](/wiki/Neanderthal "Neanderthal") fossils dating to the middle [Palaeolithic](/wiki/Palaeolithic "Palaeolithic") period were unearthed in northern Croatia, best presented at the [Krapina site](/wiki/Krapina_Neanderthal_site "Krapina Neanderthal site").[[21]](#cite_note-26) Remnants of [Neolithic](/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic") and [Chalcolithic](/wiki/Chalcolithic "Chalcolithic") cultures were found in all regions.[[22]](#cite_note-27) The largest proportion of sites is in the valleys of northern Croatia. The most significant are [Baden](/wiki/Baden_culture "Baden culture"), [Starčevo](/wiki/Star%C4%8Devo_culture "Starčevo culture"), and [Vučedol cultures](/wiki/Vu%C4%8Dedol_culture "Vučedol culture").[[23]](#cite_note-28)[[24]](#cite_note-29) [Iron Age](/wiki/Iron_Age "Iron Age") hosted the early Illyrian [Hallstatt culture](/wiki/Hallstatt_culture "Hallstatt culture") and the Celtic [La Tène culture](/wiki/La_T%C3%A8ne_culture "La Tène culture").[[25]](#cite_note-30)

The region of modern-day Croatia was settled by [Illyrians](/wiki/Illyrians "Illyrians") and [Liburnians](/wiki/Liburnians "Liburnians"), while the first Greek colonies were established on the islands of [Hvar](/wiki/Hvar "Hvar"),[[26]](#cite_note-31) [Korčula](/wiki/Kor%C4%8Dula "Korčula"), and [Vis](/wiki/Vis_(island) "Vis (island)").[[27]](#cite_note-32) In 9 AD, the territory of today's Croatia became part of the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire"). Emperor [Diocletian](/wiki/Diocletian "Diocletian") was native to the region. He [had a large palace built](/wiki/Diocletian%27s_Palace "Diocletian's Palace") in [Split](/wiki/Split,_Croatia "Split, Croatia"), to which he retired after abdicating in AD 305.[[28]](#cite_note-33)

During the 5th century, the [last de jure Western Roman Emperor](/wiki/Last_de_jure_Western_Roman_Emperor "Last de jure Western Roman Emperor") [Julius Nepos](/wiki/Julius_Nepos "Julius Nepos") ruled a small realm from the palace after fleeing Italy in 475.[[29]](#cite_note-34)

### Middle Ages

Main articles: [Duchy of Croatia](/wiki/Duchy_of_Croatia "Duchy of Croatia"), [Kingdom of Croatia (925–1102)](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Croatia_(925%E2%80%931102) "Kingdom of Croatia (925–1102)"), [Kingdom of Croatia (1102–1526)](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Croatia_(1102%E2%80%931526) "Kingdom of Croatia (1102–1526)"), and [Republic of Ragusa](/wiki/Republic_of_Ragusa "Republic of Ragusa")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/Balkans925.png/330px-Balkans925.png)](/wiki/File:Balkans925.png)

[Kingdom of Croatia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Croatia_(925%E2%80%931102) "Kingdom of Croatia (925–1102)") c. 925, during the reign of [King Tomislav](/wiki/King_Tomislav "King Tomislav")

The Roman period ends with [Avar](/wiki/Avars_(Carpathians) "Avars (Carpathians)") and [Croat](/wiki/Croat "Croat") invasions in the late 6th and first half of the 7th century and the destruction of almost all Roman towns. Roman survivors retreated to more favourable sites on the coast, islands, and mountains. The city of [Dubrovnik](/wiki/Dubrovnik "Dubrovnik") was founded by such survivors from [Epidaurum](/wiki/Epidaurum "Epidaurum").[[30]](#cite_note-AAPatton-35)

There's some uncertainty about the [ethnogenesis of Croats](/wiki/Origin_hypotheses_of_the_Croats "Origin hypotheses of the Croats"). The most accepted theory, the Slavic theory, proposes migration of [White Croats](/wiki/White_Croats "White Croats") from [White Croatia](/wiki/White_Croatia "White Croatia") during the [Migration Period](/wiki/Migration_Period "Migration Period"). Conversely, the Iranian theory proposes [Sarmatian](/wiki/Sarmatian "Sarmatian")-[Alanic](/wiki/Alans "Alans") origin of Proto-Croats, based on [Tanais Tablets](/wiki/Tanais_Tablets "Tanais Tablets") containing [Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek") inscriptions of given names *Χορούαθος*, *Χοροάθος*, and *Χορόαθος* (Khoroúathos, Khoroáthos, and Khoróathos) and their interpretation as [anthroponyms](/wiki/Anthroponym "Anthroponym") related to the [Croatian ethnonym](/wiki/Names_of_the_Croats_and_Croatia "Names of the Croats and Croatia").[[31]](#cite_note-Nikšić-Heršak-36)

According to the work *[De Administrando Imperio](/wiki/De_Administrando_Imperio "De Administrando Imperio")* written by 10th-century Byzantine Emperor [Constantine VII](/wiki/Constantine_VII "Constantine VII"), [Croats](/wiki/Croats "Croats") settled in the [Roman province of Dalmatia](/wiki/Roman_province_of_Dalmatia "Roman province of Dalmatia") in the first half of the 7th century after they defeated the [Avars](/wiki/Pannonian_Avars "Pannonian Avars").[[32]](#cite_note-37)[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoldstein199913-38)[[34]](#cite_note-39) Although there exist some scholarly disputes about the account's reliability and interpretation,[[35]](#cite_note-40)[[36]](#cite_note-41) recent archaeological data has established that the migration and settlement of the Slavs/Croats was in the late 6th and early 7th century.[[37]](#cite_note-Belos00-42)[[38]](#cite_note-43)[[39]](#cite_note-44) Eventually, a [dukedom](/wiki/Dukedom_(administrative_division) "Dukedom (administrative division)") was formed, [Duchy of Croatia](/wiki/Duchy_of_Croatia "Duchy of Croatia"), ruled by [Borna](/wiki/Borna_(duke) "Borna (duke)"), as attested by chronicles of [Einhard](/wiki/Einhard "Einhard") starting in 818. The record represents the first document of Croatian realms, [vassal states](/wiki/Vassal_states "Vassal states") of [Francia](/wiki/Francia "Francia") at the time.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMužić2007157–160-45) Its neighbor to the North was [Principality of Lower Pannonia](/wiki/Principality_of_Lower_Pannonia "Principality of Lower Pannonia"), at the time ruled by duke [Ljudevit](/wiki/Ljudevit_(Lower_Pannonia) "Ljudevit (Lower Pannonia)") who ruled the territories between the [Drava](/wiki/Drava "Drava") and [Sava](/wiki/Sava "Sava") rivers, centred from his fort at [Sisak](/wiki/Sisak "Sisak"). This population and territory throughout history was tightly related and connected to Croats and Croatia.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBudak2018178–185-46)

[Christianisation](/wiki/Christianisation "Christianisation") of Croats began in the 7th century at the time of archon [Porga of Croatia](/wiki/Porga_of_Croatia "Porga of Croatia"), initially probably encompassed only the elite and related people,[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBudak2018144–145-47) but mostly finished by the 9th century.[[43]](#cite_note-48)[[44]](#cite_note-49) The Frankish overlordship ended during the reign of [Mislav](/wiki/Mislav_of_Croatia "Mislav of Croatia"),[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMužić2007169–170-50) or his successor [Trpimir I](/wiki/Trpimir_I_of_Croatia "Trpimir I of Croatia").[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBudak2018106-51) The native Croatian royal dynasty was founded by duke Trpimir I in the mid 9th century, who defeated the Byzantine and Bulgarian forces.[[47]](#cite_note-52)

The first native Croatian ruler recognised by the Pope was duke [Branimir](/wiki/Branimir_of_Croatia "Branimir of Croatia"), who received papal recognition from [Pope John VIII](/wiki/Pope_John_VIII "Pope John VIII") on 7 June 879.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMužić2007195–198-23) [Tomislav](/wiki/Tomislav_of_Croatia "Tomislav of Croatia") was the first [king of Croatia](/wiki/King_of_Croatia "King of Croatia"), noted as such in a letter of [Pope John X](/wiki/Pope_John_X "Pope John X") in 925. Tomislav defeated Hungarian and Bulgarian invasions.[[48]](#cite_note-Posavec-53) The medieval Croatian kingdom reached its peak in the 11th century during the reigns of [Petar Krešimir IV](/wiki/Petar_Kre%C5%A1imir_IV "Petar Krešimir IV") (1058–1074) and [Dmitar Zvonimir](/wiki/Dmitar_Zvonimir "Dmitar Zvonimir") (1075–1089).[[49]](#cite_note-Margetić-54) When [Stjepan II](/wiki/Stephen_II_of_Croatia "Stephen II of Croatia") died in 1091, ending the Trpimirović dynasty, Dmitar Zvonimir's brother-in-law [Ladislaus I of Hungary](/wiki/Ladislaus_I_of_Hungary "Ladislaus I of Hungary") claimed the Croatian crown. This led to a [war](/wiki/Battle_of_Gvozd_Mountain "Battle of Gvozd Mountain") and [personal union with Hungary](/wiki/Personal_union_of_Croatia_and_Hungary "Personal union of Croatia and Hungary") in 1102 under [Coloman](/wiki/Coloman,_King_of_Hungary "Coloman, King of Hungary").[[50]](#cite_note-HR-HU-Heka-55)

### Union with Hungary and Austria

Main articles: [Kingdom of Croatia (Habsburg)](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Croatia_(Habsburg) "Kingdom of Croatia (Habsburg)") and [Austria-Hungary](/wiki/Austria-Hungary "Austria-Hungary")

Further information: [Croatian–Ottoman wars](/wiki/Croatian%E2%80%93Ottoman_wars "Croatian–Ottoman wars")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/Dragutin_Weing%C3%A4rtner%2C_Hrvatski_sabor_1848._god.jpg/330px-Dragutin_Weing%C3%A4rtner%2C_Hrvatski_sabor_1848._god.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dragutin_Weing%C3%A4rtner,_Hrvatski_sabor_1848._god.jpg)

Ban [Josip Jelačić](/wiki/Josip_Jela%C4%8Di%C4%87 "Josip Jelačić") at the opening of the first modern [Croatian Parliament](/wiki/Croatian_Parliament "Croatian Parliament") (*Sabor*), 5 June 1848. The Croatian tricolour flag can be seen in the background.

For the next four centuries, the Kingdom of Croatia was ruled by the [Sabor](/wiki/Sabor "Sabor") (parliament) and a [Ban](/wiki/Ban_of_Croatia "Ban of Croatia") (viceroy) appointed by the king.[[51]](#cite_note-Povijest-saborovanja-56) This period saw the rise of influential nobility such as the [Frankopan](/wiki/Frankopan "Frankopan") and [Šubić](/wiki/%C5%A0ubi%C4%87 "Šubić") families to prominence, and ultimately numerous Bans from the two families.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFont200517-57) An increasing threat of [Ottoman](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") conquest and a struggle against the [Republic of Venice](/wiki/Republic_of_Venice "Republic of Venice") for control of coastal areas ensued. The Venetians controlled most of Dalmatia by 1428, except the [city-state of Dubrovnik](/wiki/City-state_of_Dubrovnik "City-state of Dubrovnik"), which became independent. [Ottoman conquests](/wiki/Hundred_Years%27_Croatian%E2%80%93Ottoman_War "Hundred Years' Croatian–Ottoman War") led to the 1493 [Battle of Krbava field](/wiki/Battle_of_Krbava_field "Battle of Krbava field") and the 1526 [Battle of Mohács](/wiki/Battle_of_Moh%C3%A1cs "Battle of Mohács"), both ending in decisive Ottoman victories. King [Louis II](/wiki/Louis_II_of_Hungary "Louis II of Hungary") died at Mohács, and in 1527, the [Croatian Parliament met in Cetin](/wiki/Croatian_Parliament_met_in_Cetin "Croatian Parliament met in Cetin") and chose Ferdinand I of the [House of Habsburg](/wiki/House_of_Habsburg "House of Habsburg") as the new ruler of Croatia, under the condition that he protects Croatia against the Ottoman Empire while respecting its political rights.[[51]](#cite_note-Povijest-saborovanja-56)[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFrucht2005422–423-58)

Following the decisive Ottoman victories, Croatia was split into civilian and military territories in 1538. The military territories became known as the [Croatian Military Frontier](/wiki/Croatian_Military_Frontier "Croatian Military Frontier") and were under direct Habsburg control. Ottoman advances in Croatia continued until the 1593 [Battle of Sisak](/wiki/Battle_of_Sisak "Battle of Sisak"), the first decisive Ottoman defeat, when borders stabilised.[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFrucht2005422–423-58) During the [Great Turkish War](/wiki/Great_Turkish_War "Great Turkish War") (1683–1698), [Slavonia](/wiki/Slavonia "Slavonia") was regained, but western [Bosnia](/wiki/Bosnia_(region) "Bosnia (region)"), which had been part of Croatia before the Ottoman conquest, remained outside Croatian control.[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFrucht2005422–423-58) The present-day border between the two countries is a remnant of this outcome. [Dalmatia](/wiki/Dalmatia "Dalmatia"), the southern part of the border, was similarly defined by the [Fifth](/wiki/Cretan_War_(1645%E2%80%931669) "Cretan War (1645–1669)") and the [Seventh Ottoman–Venetian Wars](/wiki/Seventh_Ottoman%E2%80%93Venetian_Wars "Seventh Ottoman–Venetian Wars").[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELane1973409-59)

The [Ottoman wars](/wiki/Croatian%E2%80%93Ottoman_wars "Croatian–Ottoman wars") drove demographic changes. During the 16th century, Croats from western and northern [Bosnia](/wiki/Bosnia_(region) "Bosnia (region)"), [Lika](/wiki/Lika "Lika"), [Krbava](/wiki/Krbava "Krbava"), the area between the rivers [Una](/wiki/Una_(Sava) "Una (Sava)") and [Kupa](/wiki/Kupa "Kupa"), and especially from western [Slavonia](/wiki/Slavonia "Slavonia"), migrated towards [Austria](/wiki/Austria "Austria"). Present-day [Burgenland Croats](/wiki/Burgenland_Croats "Burgenland Croats") are direct descendants of these settlers.[[55]](#cite_note-60)[[56]](#cite_note-61) To replace the fleeing population, the Habsburgs encouraged Bosnians to provide military service in the [Military Frontier](/wiki/Military_Frontier "Military Frontier").

The [Croatian Parliament](/wiki/Croatian_Parliament "Croatian Parliament") supported [King Charles III](/wiki/Emperor_Charles_VI "Emperor Charles VI")'s [Pragmatic Sanction](/wiki/Pragmatic_Sanction_of_1713 "Pragmatic Sanction of 1713") and signed their own [Pragmatic Sanction in 1712](/wiki/Pragmatic_Sanction_in_1712 "Pragmatic Sanction in 1712").[[57]](#cite_note-62) Subsequently, the emperor pledged to respect all privileges and political rights of the [Kingdom of Croatia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Croatia_(Habsburg) "Kingdom of Croatia (Habsburg)"), and [Queen Maria Theresa](/wiki/Queen_Maria_Theresa "Queen Maria Theresa") made significant contributions to Croatian affairs, such as introducing compulsory education.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/Map_of_the_Kingdom_of_Croatia-Slavonia_%281885%29.png/330px-Map_of_the_Kingdom_of_Croatia-Slavonia_%281885%29.png)](/wiki/File:Map_of_the_Kingdom_of_Croatia-Slavonia_(1885).png)

The [Kingdom of Croatia-Slavonia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Croatia-Slavonia "Kingdom of Croatia-Slavonia") was an autonomous kingdom within [Austria-Hungary](/wiki/Austria-Hungary "Austria-Hungary") created in 1868 following the [Croatian–Hungarian Settlement](/wiki/Croatian%E2%80%93Hungarian_Settlement "Croatian–Hungarian Settlement").

Between 1797 and 1809, the [First French Empire](/wiki/First_French_Empire "First French Empire") increasingly occupied the eastern [Adriatic](/wiki/Adriatic "Adriatic") coastline and its hinterland, ending the Venetian and the [Ragusan republics](/wiki/Republic_of_Ragusa "Republic of Ragusa"), establishing the [Illyrian Provinces](/wiki/Illyrian_Provinces "Illyrian Provinces").[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFrucht2005422–423-58) In response, the [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy") [blockaded the Adriatic Sea](/wiki/Blockade_of_the_Adriatic_Sea "Blockade of the Adriatic Sea"), leading to the [Battle of Vis](/wiki/Battle_of_Lissa_(1811) "Battle of Lissa (1811)") in 1811.[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAdkinsAdkins2008359–362-63) The Illyrian provinces were captured by the Austrians in 1813 and absorbed by the [Austrian Empire](/wiki/Austrian_Empire "Austrian Empire") following the [Congress of Vienna](/wiki/Congress_of_Vienna "Congress of Vienna") in 1815. This led to the formation of the [Kingdom of Dalmatia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Dalmatia "Kingdom of Dalmatia") and the restoration of the [Croatian Littoral](/wiki/Croatian_Littoral "Croatian Littoral") to the Kingdom of Croatia under one crown.[[59]](#cite_note-64) The 1830s and 1840s featured [romantic nationalism](/wiki/Romantic_nationalism "Romantic nationalism") that inspired the [Croatian National Revival](/wiki/Croatian_National_Revival "Croatian National Revival"), a political and cultural campaign advocating the unity of [South Slavs](/wiki/South_Slavs "South Slavs") within the empire. Its primary focus was establishing a standard language as a counterweight to [Hungarian](/wiki/Hungarian_language "Hungarian language") while promoting [Croatian literature](/wiki/Croatian_literature "Croatian literature") and culture.[[60]](#cite_note-CRIS-Stančić-65) During the [Hungarian Revolution of 1848](/wiki/Hungarian_Revolution_of_1848 "Hungarian Revolution of 1848"), Croatia sided with Austria. Ban [Josip Jelačić](/wiki/Josip_Jela%C4%8Di%C4%87 "Josip Jelačić") helped defeat the Hungarians in 1849 and ushered in a [Germanisation](/wiki/Germanisation "Germanisation") policy.[[61]](#cite_note-66) This is around the time that [Nikola Tesla](/wiki/Nikola_Tesla "Nikola Tesla") was born in [Smiljan](/wiki/Smiljan,_Croatia "Smiljan, Croatia").

By the 1860s, the failure of the policy became apparent, leading to the [Austro-Hungarian Compromise of 1867](/wiki/Austro-Hungarian_Compromise_of_1867 "Austro-Hungarian Compromise of 1867"). The creation of a [personal union](/wiki/Personal_union "Personal union") between the Austrian Empire and the [Kingdom of Hungary](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Hungary "Kingdom of Hungary") followed. The treaty left Croatia's status to Hungary, which was resolved by the [Croatian–Hungarian Settlement](/wiki/Croatian%E2%80%93Hungarian_Settlement "Croatian–Hungarian Settlement") of 1868 when the kingdoms of Croatia and Slavonia were united.[[62]](#cite_note-67) The Kingdom of Dalmatia remained under de facto Austrian control, while [Rijeka](/wiki/Rijeka "Rijeka") retained the status of [*corpus separatum*](/wiki/Corpus_separatum_(Fiume) "Corpus separatum (Fiume)") previously introduced in 1779.[[50]](#cite_note-HR-HU-Heka-55)

After [Austria-Hungary](/wiki/Austria-Hungary "Austria-Hungary") [occupied Bosnia and Herzegovina](/wiki/Austro-Hungarian_rule_in_Bosnia_and_Herzegovina "Austro-Hungarian rule in Bosnia and Herzegovina") following the [1878 Treaty of Berlin](/wiki/1878_Treaty_of_Berlin "1878 Treaty of Berlin"), the Military Frontier was abolished. The Croatian and [Slavonian](/wiki/Slavonian_Military_Frontier "Slavonian Military Frontier") sectors of the Frontier returned to Croatia in 1881,[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFrucht2005422–423-58) under provisions of the Croatian–Hungarian Settlement.[[63]](#cite_note-68)[[64]](#cite_note-Dubravica-69) Renewed efforts to [reform Austria-Hungary](/wiki/Reform_Austria-Hungary "Reform Austria-Hungary"), entailing [federalisation](/wiki/Federalisation "Federalisation") with Croatia as a federal unit, were stopped by [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I").[[65]](#cite_note-70)

### The World Wars and Yugoslavia

Main articles: [Creation of Yugoslavia](/wiki/Creation_of_Yugoslavia "Creation of Yugoslavia"), [Kingdom of Yugoslavia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Yugoslavia "Kingdom of Yugoslavia"), [Banovina of Croatia](/wiki/Banovina_of_Croatia "Banovina of Croatia"), [World War II in Yugoslavia](/wiki/World_War_II_in_Yugoslavia "World War II in Yugoslavia"), and [Socialist Republic of Croatia](/wiki/Socialist_Republic_of_Croatia "Socialist Republic of Croatia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/Demonstracije_u_Zagrebu_1918.jpg/250px-Demonstracije_u_Zagrebu_1918.jpg)](/wiki/File:Demonstracije_u_Zagrebu_1918.jpg)

Mass protests in Zagreb against the unification of the [State of Slovenes, Croats and Serbs](/wiki/State_of_Slovenes,_Croats_and_Serbs "State of Slovenes, Croats and Serbs") with the [Kingdom of Serbia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Serbia "Kingdom of Serbia") in 1918

On 29 October 1918, the Croatian Parliament (*Sabor*) declared independence and decided to join the newly formed [State of Slovenes, Croats, and Serbs](/wiki/State_of_Slovenes,_Croats,_and_Serbs "State of Slovenes, Croats, and Serbs"),[[51]](#cite_note-Povijest-saborovanja-56) which in turn entered into union with the [Kingdom of Serbia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Serbia "Kingdom of Serbia") on 4 December 1918 to form the [Kingdom of Serbs, Croats, and Slovenes](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Serbs,_Croats,_and_Slovenes "Kingdom of Serbs, Croats, and Slovenes").[[66]](#cite_note-71) The Croatian Parliament never ratified the union with Serbia and Montenegro.[[51]](#cite_note-Povijest-saborovanja-56) The [1921 constitution](/wiki/1921_constitution "1921 constitution") defining the country as a [unitary state](/wiki/Unitary_state "Unitary state") and abolition of Croatian Parliament and historical administrative divisions effectively ended Croatian autonomy.

The new constitution was opposed by the most widely supported national political party—the [Croatian Peasant Party](/wiki/Croatian_Peasant_Party "Croatian Peasant Party") (HSS) led by [Stjepan Radić](/wiki/Stjepan_Radi%C4%87 "Stjepan Radić").[[67]](#cite_note-72)

The political situation deteriorated further as Radić was assassinated in the [National Assembly](/wiki/Yugoslav_Parliament "Yugoslav Parliament") by [NRS](/wiki/People%27s_Radical_Party "People's Radical Party") member, [Serbian](/wiki/Serbs "Serbs") nationalist politician [Puniša Račić](/wiki/Puni%C5%A1a_Ra%C4%8Di%C4%87 "Puniša Račić") in 1928, culminating in King [Alexander I](/wiki/Alexander_I_of_Yugoslavia "Alexander I of Yugoslavia")'s establishment of the [6 January Dictatorship](/wiki/6_January_Dictatorship "6 January Dictatorship") in 1929.[[68]](#cite_note-73) The dictatorship formally ended in 1931 when the king imposed a more unitary constitution.[[69]](#cite_note-74) The HSS, now led by [Vladko Maček](/wiki/Vladko_Ma%C4%8Dek "Vladko Maček"), continued to advocate federalisation, resulting in the [Cvetković–Maček Agreement](/wiki/Cvetkovi%C4%87%E2%80%93Ma%C4%8Dek_Agreement "Cvetković–Maček Agreement") of August 1939 and the autonomous [Banovina of Croatia](/wiki/Banovina_of_Croatia "Banovina of Croatia"). The Yugoslav government retained control of defence, internal security, foreign affairs, trade, and transport while other matters were left to the Croatian Sabor and a crown-appointed Ban.[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKlemenčičŽagar2004121–123-75)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/Oslobo%C4%91enje_Zagreba_05.jpg/250px-Oslobo%C4%91enje_Zagreba_05.jpg)](/wiki/File:Oslobo%C4%91enje_Zagreba_05.jpg)

People of Zagreb celebrating [liberation](/wiki/Zagreb_in_World_War_II "Zagreb in World War II") on 12 May 1945 by [Croatian Partisans](/wiki/Croatian_Partisans "Croatian Partisans")

In April 1941, [Yugoslavia was invaded](/wiki/Invasion_of_Yugoslavia "Invasion of Yugoslavia") by [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") and [Fascist Italy](/wiki/Fascist_Italy_(1922%E2%80%931943) "Fascist Italy (1922–1943)"). Following the invasion, a German-Italian installed puppet state named the [Independent State of Croatia](/wiki/Independent_State_of_Croatia "Independent State of Croatia") (NDH) was established. Most of Croatia, Bosnia and Herzegovina, and the region of [Syrmia](/wiki/Syrmia "Syrmia") were incorporated into this state. Parts of Dalmatia were [annexed by Italy](/wiki/Treaties_of_Rome_(1941) "Treaties of Rome (1941)"), Hungary annexed the northern Croatian regions of [Baranja](/wiki/Baranya_(region) "Baranya (region)") and [Međimurje](/wiki/Me%C4%91imurje_(region) "Međimurje (region)").[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKlemenčičŽagar2004153–156-76) The NDH regime was led by [Ante Pavelić](/wiki/Ante_Paveli%C4%87 "Ante Pavelić") and the ultranationalist [Ustaše](/wiki/Usta%C5%A1e "Ustaše"), a fringe movement in pre-war Croatia.[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETomasevich2001337-77) With German and Italian military and political support,[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETomasevich2001272-78) the regime introduced [racial laws](/wiki/Nazi_racial_theories "Nazi racial theories") and launched a [genocide campaign against Serbs](/wiki/Genocide_of_Serbs_in_the_Independent_State_of_Croatia "Genocide of Serbs in the Independent State of Croatia"), [Jews](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Croatia "History of the Jews in Croatia"), and [Roma](/wiki/Romani_people_in_Croatia "Romani people in Croatia").[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKlemenčičŽagar2004184-79) Many were imprisoned in [concentration camps](/wiki/Concentration_camps_in_the_Independent_State_of_Croatia "Concentration camps in the Independent State of Croatia"); the largest was the [Jasenovac complex](/wiki/Jasenovac_concentration_camp "Jasenovac concentration camp").[[75]](#cite_note-logori-80) [Anti-fascist Croats](/wiki/Croatian_Partisans "Croatian Partisans") were targeted by the regime as well.[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoldstein1999138-81) Several [concentration camps](/wiki/Italian_concentration_camps "Italian concentration camps") (most notably the [Rab](/wiki/Rab_concentration_camp "Rab concentration camp"), [Gonars](/wiki/Gonars_concentration_camp "Gonars concentration camp") and [Molat](/wiki/Molat_concentration_camp "Molat concentration camp") camps) were established in Italian-occupied territories, mostly for [Slovenes](/wiki/Slovenes "Slovenes") and Croats.[[75]](#cite_note-logori-80) At the same time, the Yugoslav Royalist and [Serbian nationalist](/wiki/Serbian_nationalism "Serbian nationalism") [Chetniks](/wiki/Chetniks "Chetniks") pursued a [genocidal campaign](/wiki/Chetnik_war_crimes_in_World_War_II "Chetnik war crimes in World War II") against Croats and [Muslims](/wiki/Bosniaks "Bosniaks"),[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKlemenčičŽagar2004184-79)[[77]](#cite_note-82) aided by Italy.[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETomasevich2001744-83) Nazi German forces committed crimes and reprisals against civilians in retaliation for Partisan actions, such as in the villages of [Kamešnica](/wiki/Massacre_of_villages_under_Kame%C5%A1nica "Massacre of villages under Kamešnica") and [Lipa](/wiki/Memorial_Centre_Lipa_Remembers "Memorial Centre Lipa Remembers") in 1944.[[79]](#cite_note-84)[[80]](#cite_note-85)

A [resistance movement](/wiki/National_Liberation_Movement_in_Croatia "National Liberation Movement in Croatia") emerged. On 22 June 1941,[[81]](#cite_note-Pavličević-86) the [1st Sisak Partisan Detachment](/wiki/1st_Sisak_Partisan_Detachment "1st Sisak Partisan Detachment") was formed near [Sisak](/wiki/Sisak "Sisak"), the first military unit formed by a resistance movement in [occupied Europe](/wiki/German-occupied_Europe "German-occupied Europe").[[82]](#cite_note-pavlicevic-2007-87) That sparked the beginning of the [Yugoslav Partisan](/wiki/Yugoslav_Partisan "Yugoslav Partisan") movement, a communist, multi-ethnic anti-fascist resistance group led by [Josip Broz Tito](/wiki/Josip_Broz_Tito "Josip Broz Tito").[[83]](#cite_note-88) In ethnic terms, Croats were the second-largest contributors to the Partisan movement after Serbs.[[84]](#cite_note-Hoare_2011-89) In per capita terms, Croats contributed proportionately to their population within Yugoslavia.[[85]](#cite_note-90) By May 1944 (according to Tito), Croats made up 30% of the Partisan's ethnic composition, despite making up 22% of the population.[[84]](#cite_note-Hoare_2011-89) The movement grew fast, and at the [Tehran Conference](/wiki/Tehran_Conference "Tehran Conference") in December 1943, the Partisans gained recognition from the [Allies](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_II "Allies of World War II").[[86]](#cite_note-91)

With Allied support in logistics, equipment, training and airpower, and with the assistance of [Soviet troops](/wiki/Red_Army "Red Army") taking part in the 1944 [Belgrade Offensive](/wiki/Belgrade_Offensive "Belgrade Offensive"), the Partisans gained control of Yugoslavia and the border regions of [Italy](/wiki/Trieste#World_War_II_and_aftermath "Trieste") and [Austria](/wiki/Carinthia_(state)#From_1920_to_the_Present "Carinthia (state)") by May 1945. Members of the [NDH armed forces](/wiki/Croatian_Armed_Forces_(Independent_State_of_Croatia) "Croatian Armed Forces (Independent State of Croatia)") and other Axis troops, as well as civilians, were in retreat towards Austria. Following their surrender, many were killed in the [Yugoslav death march of Nazi collaborators](/wiki/Yugoslav_death_march_of_Nazi_collaborators "Yugoslav death march of Nazi collaborators").[[87]](#cite_note-92) In the following years, [ethnic Germans](/wiki/Germans_of_Croatia "Germans of Croatia") faced [persecution in Yugoslavia](/wiki/Flight_and_expulsion_of_Germans_(1944%E2%80%9350)#Yugoslavia "Flight and expulsion of Germans (1944–50)"), and many were interned.[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoldstein1999158-93)

The political aspirations of the Partisan movement were reflected in the [State Anti-fascist Council for the National Liberation of Croatia](/wiki/State_Anti-fascist_Council_for_the_National_Liberation_of_Croatia "State Anti-fascist Council for the National Liberation of Croatia"), which developed in 1943 as the bearer of Croatian statehood and later transformed into the Parliament in 1945, and [AVNOJ](/wiki/AVNOJ "AVNOJ")—its counterpart at the Yugoslav level.[[89]](#cite_note-94)[[90]](#cite_note-95)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/Event_in_Zagreb_in_honour_of_Tito.jpg/250px-Event_in_Zagreb_in_honour_of_Tito.jpg)](/wiki/File:Event_in_Zagreb_in_honour_of_Tito.jpg)

Cardinal [Aloysius Stepinac](/wiki/Aloysius_Stepinac "Aloysius Stepinac") with the Croatian communist leader [Vladimir Bakarić](/wiki/Vladimir_Bakari%C4%87 "Vladimir Bakarić") at the celebration of [May Day](/wiki/International_Workers%27_Day "International Workers' Day"), shortly before Stepinac was arrested and convicted by the communists

Based on the studies on [wartime and post-war casualties](/wiki/World_War_II_in_Yugoslavia_casualties "World War II in Yugoslavia casualties") by demographer [Vladimir Žerjavić](/wiki/Vladimir_%C5%BDerjavi%C4%87 "Vladimir Žerjavić") and statistician [Bogoljub Kočović](/wiki/Bogoljub_Ko%C4%8Dovi%C4%87 "Bogoljub Kočović"), a total of 295,000 people from the territory (not including territories [ceded from Italy](/wiki/Treaty_of_Peace_with_Italy,_1947 "Treaty of Peace with Italy, 1947") after the war) died, which amounted to 7.3% of the population,[[91]](#cite_note-96) among whom were 125–137,000 Serbs, 118–124,000 Croats, 16–17,000 Jews, and 15,000 Roma.[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEŽerjavić1992159-97)[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKočović1985173-98) In addition, from areas joined to Croatia after the war, a total of 32,000 people died, among whom 16,000 were Italians and 15,000 were Croats.[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEŽerjavić1993b640–641-99) Approximately 200,000 Croats from the entirety of Yugoslavia (including Croatia) and abroad were killed in total throughout the war and its immediate aftermath, approximately 5.4% of the population.[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKočović1985126-100)[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGeiger2012117–118-101)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Nixontito19712.jpg/250px-Nixontito19712.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nixontito19712.jpg)

[Josip Broz Tito](/wiki/Josip_Broz_Tito "Josip Broz Tito") led [Yugoslavia](/wiki/Socialist_Federal_Republic_of_Yugoslavia "Socialist Federal Republic of Yugoslavia") from 1944 to 1980; Pictured: Tito with the US president [Richard Nixon](/wiki/Richard_Nixon "Richard Nixon") in the [White House](/wiki/White_House "White House"), 1971

After [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), Croatia became a [single-party](/wiki/Single-party "Single-party") socialist [federal unit](/wiki/Socialist_Republic_of_Croatia "Socialist Republic of Croatia") of the [SFR Yugoslavia](/wiki/Socialist_Federal_Republic_of_Yugoslavia "Socialist Federal Republic of Yugoslavia"), ruled by the [Communists](/wiki/League_of_Communists_of_Croatia "League of Communists of Croatia"), but having a degree of autonomy within the federation. In 1967, Croatian authors and linguists published a [Declaration on the Status and Name of the Croatian Standard Language](/wiki/Declaration_on_the_Status_and_Name_of_the_Croatian_Standard_Language "Declaration on the Status and Name of the Croatian Standard Language") demanding equal treatment for their language.[[97]](#cite_note-Šute-Deklaracija-102)

The declaration contributed to a national movement seeking greater civil rights and redistribution of the Yugoslav economy, culminating in the [Croatian Spring](/wiki/Croatian_Spring "Croatian Spring") of 1971, which was suppressed by Yugoslav leadership.[[98]](#cite_note-JL-Savka-103) Still, the [1974 Yugoslav Constitution](/wiki/1974_Yugoslav_Constitution "1974 Yugoslav Constitution") gave increased autonomy to federal units, basically fulfilling a goal of the Croatian Spring and providing a legal basis for independence of the federative constituents.[[99]](#cite_note-Rich-104) At the same time, there was a substantial Croatian diaspora during the Cold War, which included efforts aimed at forming a government in exile.[[100]](#cite_note-Mišur-105)

Following Tito's death in 1980, the political situation in Yugoslavia deteriorated. National tension was fanned by the 1986 [SANU Memorandum](/wiki/SANU_Memorandum "SANU Memorandum") and the [1989 coups in Vojvodina, Kosovo, and Montenegro](/wiki/1989_coups_in_Vojvodina,_Kosovo_and_Montenegro "1989 coups in Vojvodina, Kosovo and Montenegro").[[101]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFrucht2005433-106)[[102]](#cite_note-107) In January 1990, the Communist Party fragmented along national lines, with the Croatian [faction](/wiki/Political_faction "Political faction") demanding a looser federation.[[103]](#cite_note-Pauković-14Congress-2009-108) In the same year, the [first multi-party elections](/wiki/1990_Croatian_parliamentary_election "1990 Croatian parliamentary election") were held in Croatia, while [Franjo Tuđman](/wiki/Franjo_Tu%C4%91man "Franjo Tuđman")'s win exacerbated nationalist tensions.[[104]](#cite_note-Independent-Tuđman-Obituary-109) Some of the [Serbs in Croatia](/wiki/Serbs_in_Croatia "Serbs in Croatia") left Sabor and declared autonomy of the unrecognised [Republic of Serbian Krajina](/wiki/Republic_of_Serbian_Krajina "Republic of Serbian Krajina"), intent on achieving independence from Croatia.[[105]](#cite_note-NYTimes-Autonomy-AUG1990-110)[[106]](#cite_note-EE-CIS-book-111)

### Independence

Main articles: [Breakup of Yugoslavia](/wiki/Breakup_of_Yugoslavia "Breakup of Yugoslavia"), [Croatian War of Independence](/wiki/Croatian_War_of_Independence "Croatian War of Independence"), [Independence of Croatia](/wiki/Independence_of_Croatia "Independence of Croatia"), and [History of Croatia since 1995](/wiki/History_of_Croatia_since_1995 "History of Croatia since 1995")

As tensions rose, Croatia [declared independence](/wiki/Independence_of_Croatia "Independence of Croatia") on 25 June 1991. However, the full implementation of the declaration only came into effect after a [three-month moratorium on the decision](/wiki/Brioni_Agreement "Brioni Agreement") on 8 October 1991.[[107]](#cite_note-NYTimes-Declaration-26June1991-112)[[108]](#cite_note-Sabor-Independence-8Oct1991-113) In the meantime, tensions escalated into [overt war](/wiki/Croatian_War_of_Independence "Croatian War of Independence") when the Serbian-controlled [Yugoslav People's Army](/wiki/Yugoslav_People%27s_Army "Yugoslav People's Army") (JNA) and various Serb paramilitary groups attacked Croatia.[[109]](#cite_note-NYTimes-Otkos10-114)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/Vukovar_Memorial_Cemetery_%E2%80%93_Eternal_Flame%2C_20150429160745.jpg/250px-Vukovar_Memorial_Cemetery_%E2%80%93_Eternal_Flame%2C_20150429160745.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vukovar_Memorial_Cemetery_%E2%80%93_Eternal_Flame,_20150429160745.jpg)

The [National Memorial Cemetery of The Victims of Homeland War in Vukovar](/wiki/National_Memorial_Cemetery_of_The_Victims_of_Homeland_War_in_Vukovar "National Memorial Cemetery of The Victims of Homeland War in Vukovar") — the central site of the [National Remembrance Day](/wiki/Remembrance_Day_(Croatia) "Remembrance Day (Croatia)"), commemorating all war victims and the [Vukovar massacre](/wiki/Vukovar_massacre "Vukovar massacre"), a pivotal event in [Croatia's fight for independence 1991](/wiki/Croatian_War_of_Independence "Croatian War of Independence").

By the end of 1991, a high-intensity conflict fought along a wide front reduced Croatia's control to about two-thirds of its territory.[[110]](#cite_note-NYTimes-Iceland-115)[[111]](#cite_note-LATimes-OccupiedPct-1Aug91-116) Serb paramilitary groups then began a campaign of killing, terror, and expulsion of the Croats in the occupied territories, killing thousands[[112]](#cite_note-117) of Croat civilians and expelling or displacing as many as 400,000–500,000 Croats and other non-Serbs from their homes.[[113]](#cite_note-118)[[114]](#cite_note-Al_Jazeera_2020-119) Serbs living in Croatian towns, especially those near the front lines, were subjected to various forms of discrimination.[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoldstein1999233-120) Croatian Serbs in Eastern and Western Slavonia and parts of the Krajina were forced to flee or were expelled by Croatian forces, though on a restricted scale and in lesser numbers.[[116]](#cite_note-121) The [Croatian Government](/wiki/Government_of_Croatia "Government of Croatia") publicly deplored these practices and sought to stop them, indicating that they were not a part of the Government's policy.[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen199646-122)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/16_obljetnica_vojnoredarstvene_operacije_Oluja_05082011_Kninska_tvrdjava_155.jpg/330px-16_obljetnica_vojnoredarstvene_operacije_Oluja_05082011_Kninska_tvrdjava_155.jpg)](/wiki/File:16_obljetnica_vojnoredarstvene_operacije_Oluja_05082011_Kninska_tvrdjava_155.jpg)

[Croatian soldiers](/wiki/Army_of_Croatia "Army of Croatia") raising the flag on the [Knin fortress](/wiki/Knin_fortress "Knin fortress") at a commemoration of the [Operation Storm](/wiki/Operation_Storm "Operation Storm"), the Croatian military action which liberated occupied Croatian territories in 1995

On 15 January 1992, Croatia gained [diplomatic recognition](/wiki/Diplomatic_recognition "Diplomatic recognition") by the [European Economic Community](/wiki/European_Economic_Community "European Economic Community"), followed by the United Nations.[[118]](#cite_note-NYTimes-Germany-23Dec91-123)[[119]](#cite_note-NYT-UN-membership-124) The war effectively ended in August 1995 with a [decisive victory](/wiki/Operation_Storm "Operation Storm") by Croatia;[[120]](#cite_note-LATimes-Storm-Complete-125) the event is commemorated each year on 5 August as [Victory and Homeland Thanksgiving Day and the Day of Croatian Defenders](/wiki/Victory_Day_(Croatia) "Victory Day (Croatia)").[[121]](#cite_note-126) Following the Croatian victory, about 200,000 Serbs from the self-proclaimed [Republic of Serbian Krajina](/wiki/Republic_of_Serbian_Krajina "Republic of Serbian Krajina") fled the region[[122]](#cite_note-bbc-storm-127) and hundreds of mainly elderly Serb civilians were killed in the aftermath of the military operation, often in revenge attacks, accompanied by looting, seizing or burning down of their property.[[123]](#cite_note-128) Approximately half have returned since then.[[114]](#cite_note-Al_Jazeera_2020-119) Some 19,000 Serb homes were subsequently settled by Croat refugees from Bosnia and Herzegovina.[[124]](#cite_note-129) The [remaining occupied areas](/wiki/Eastern_Slavonia,_Baranja_and_Western_Syrmia_(1995%E2%80%931998) "Eastern Slavonia, Baranja and Western Syrmia (1995–1998)") were restored to Croatia following the [Erdut Agreement](/wiki/Erdut_Agreement "Erdut Agreement") of November 1995, concluding with the [UNTAES](/wiki/United_Nations_Transitional_Administration_for_Eastern_Slavonia,_Baranja_and_Western_Sirmium "United Nations Transitional Administration for Eastern Slavonia, Baranja and Western Sirmium") mission in January 1998.[[125]](#cite_note-NYTimes-UNTAES-16Jan98-130) Most sources number the war deaths at around 20,000.[[126]](#cite_note-131)[[127]](#cite_note-setimes1-132)[[128]](#cite_note-tehrantimes1-133)

The 2000s were characterized by democratization, economic growth, structural and social reforms, and problems such as unemployment, corruption, and the inefficiency of public administration.[[129]](#cite_note-134) In November 2000 and March 2001, the Parliament amended the Constitution, first adopted on 22 December 1990, changing its bicameral structure back into its historic unicameral form and reducing presidential powers.[[130]](#cite_note-135)[[131]](#cite_note-136)

Croatia joined the [Partnership for Peace](/wiki/Partnership_for_Peace "Partnership for Peace") on 25 May 2000[[132]](#cite_note-137) and became a member of the [World Trade Organization](/wiki/World_Trade_Organization "World Trade Organization") on 30 November 2000.[[133]](#cite_note-138) On 29 October 2001, Croatia signed a [Stabilisation and Association Agreement](/wiki/Stabilisation_and_Association_Agreement "Stabilisation and Association Agreement") with the European Union,[[134]](#cite_note-139) submitted a formal application for the EU membership in 2003,[[135]](#cite_note-140) was given the status of a candidate country in 2004,[[136]](#cite_note-141) and began [accession negotiations](/wiki/2013_enlargement_of_the_European_Union#Negotiation_progress "2013 enlargement of the European Union") in 2005.[[137]](#cite_note-142) Although the Croatian economy enjoyed a significant boom in the early 2000s, the [2008 financial crisis](/wiki/2008_financial_crisis "2008 financial crisis") forced the government to cut spending, thus provoking a public outcry.[[138]](#cite_note-143)

Croatia served on the [United Nations Security Council](/wiki/United_Nations_Security_Council "United Nations Security Council") in the 2008–2009 term for the first time, assuming the non-permanent seat in December 2008.[[139]](#cite_note-MVPEI-UNSC-2008-2009-144) On 1 April 2009, Croatia joined [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO").[[140]](#cite_note-145)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/Tratado_de_Lisboa_13_12_2007_%28081%29.jpg/250px-Tratado_de_Lisboa_13_12_2007_%28081%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tratado_de_Lisboa_13_12_2007_(081).jpg)

Croatia became the 28th EU member country on 1 July 2013.

A wave of anti-government protests in 2011 reflected a general dissatisfaction with the current political and economic situation. The protests brought together diverse political persuasions in response to recent government corruption scandals and called for early elections. On 28 October 2011 [MPs](/wiki/List_of_MPs_elected_in_the_Croatian_parliamentary_election,_2007 "List of MPs elected in the Croatian parliamentary election, 2007") voted to dissolve Parliament and the protests gradually subsided. [President](/wiki/President_of_Croatia "President of Croatia") Ivo Josipović agreed to a dissolution of [Sabor](/wiki/Croatian_Parliament "Croatian Parliament") on Monday, 31 October and scheduled new elections for Sunday 4 December 2011.[[141]](#cite_note-146)[[142]](#cite_note-147)[[143]](#cite_note-148)

On 30 June 2011, Croatia successfully completed EU accession negotiations.[[144]](#cite_note-EU-Negotiations-Completed-149) The country signed the [Accession Treaty](/wiki/Treaty_of_Accession_2011 "Treaty of Accession 2011") on 9 December 2011 and held a [referendum](/wiki/2012_Croatian_European_Union_membership_referendum "2012 Croatian European Union membership referendum") on 22 January 2012, where Croatian citizens voted in favor of an EU membership.[[145]](#cite_note-EU-Accession-Treaty-150)[[146]](#cite_note-151) [Croatia joined the European Union](/wiki/Croatia_joined_the_European_Union "Croatia joined the European Union") on 1 July 2013.

Croatia was affected by the [2015 European migrant crisis](/wiki/2015_European_migrant_crisis "2015 European migrant crisis") when Hungary's closure of borders with Serbia pushed over 700,000 refugees and migrants to pass through Croatia on their way to other EU countries.[[147]](#cite_note-152)

On 19 October 2016, [Andrej Plenković](/wiki/Andrej_Plenkovi%C4%87 "Andrej Plenković") began serving as the current Croatian Prime Minister.[[148]](#cite_note-153) The most recent presidential elections, held on 12 January 2025, elected [Zoran Milanović](/wiki/Zoran_Milanovi%C4%87 "Zoran Milanović") as president.[[149]](#cite_note-154)

On 25 January 2022, the [OECD](/wiki/OECD "OECD") Council decided to open accession negotiations with Croatia. Throughout the accession process, Croatia was to implement numerous reforms that will advance all spheres of activity – from public services and the justice system to education, transport, finance, health, and trade. In line with the OECD Accession Roadmap from June 2022, Croatia will undergo technical reviews by 25 OECD committees and is so far progressing at a faster pace than expected. Full membership was expected in 2025 and is the last big foreign policy goal Croatia still has to achieve.[[150]](#cite_note-155)[[151]](#cite_note-156)[[152]](#cite_note-157)[[153]](#cite_note-158)

On 1 January 2023, Croatia adopted the [euro](/wiki/Euro "Euro") as its official currency, replacing the [kuna](/wiki/Croatian_kuna "Croatian kuna"), and became the 20th [Eurozone](/wiki/Eurozone "Eurozone") member. On the same day, Croatia became the 27th member of the border-free [Schengen Area](/wiki/Schengen_Area "Schengen Area"), thus marking its full EU integration.[[154]](#cite_note-159)

## Geography

Main article: [Geography of Croatia](/wiki/Geography_of_Croatia "Geography of Croatia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/Satellite_image_of_Croatia_in_September_2003.jpg/330px-Satellite_image_of_Croatia_in_September_2003.jpg)](/wiki/File:Satellite_image_of_Croatia_in_September_2003.jpg)

[Satellite image of Croatia](/wiki/Geography_of_Croatia "Geography of Croatia")

Croatia is situated in [Central](/wiki/Central_Europe "Central Europe") and Southeast Europe, on the coast of the [Adriatic Sea](/wiki/Adriatic_Sea "Adriatic Sea"). Hungary is to the northeast, [Serbia](/wiki/Serbia "Serbia") to the east, [Bosnia and Herzegovina](/wiki/Bosnia_and_Herzegovina "Bosnia and Herzegovina") and [Montenegro](/wiki/Montenegro "Montenegro") to the southeast and [Slovenia](/wiki/Slovenia "Slovenia") to the northwest. It lies mostly between latitudes [42°](/wiki/42nd_parallel_north "42nd parallel north") and [47° N](/wiki/47%C2%B0_N "47° N") and longitudes [13°](/wiki/13th_meridian_east "13th meridian east") and [20° E](/wiki/20%C2%B0_E "20° E").[[155]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTE2018_Statistical_Yearbook_of_the_Republic_of_Croatia44-160) Part of the territory in the extreme south surrounding [Dubrovnik](/wiki/Dubrovnik "Dubrovnik") is a [practical exclave](/wiki/Practical_exclave "Practical exclave") connected to the rest of the mainland by [territorial waters](/wiki/Territorial_waters "Territorial waters"), but separated on land by a short coastline strip belonging to Bosnia and Herzegovina around [Neum](/wiki/Neum "Neum"). The [Pelješac Bridge](/wiki/Pelje%C5%A1ac_Bridge "Pelješac Bridge") connects the exclave with mainland Croatia.[[156]](#cite_note-161)

The territory covers 56,594 square kilometres (21,851 square miles), consisting of 56,414 square kilometres (21,782 square miles) of land and 128 square kilometres (49 square miles) of water. It is the world's 127th largest country.[[157]](#cite_note-CIA-162) Elevation ranges from the mountains of the [Dinaric Alps](/wiki/Dinaric_Alps "Dinaric Alps") with the highest point of the [Dinara](/wiki/Dinara "Dinara") peak at 1,831 metres (6,007 feet) near the border with Bosnia and Herzegovina in the south[[157]](#cite_note-CIA-162) to the shore of the Adriatic Sea which makes up its entire southwest border. Insular Croatia consists of over [a thousand islands and islets](/wiki/A_thousand_islands_and_islets "A thousand islands and islets") varying in size, 48 of which are [permanently inhabited](/wiki/List_of_inhabited_islands_of_Croatia "List of inhabited islands of Croatia"). The largest islands are [Cres](/wiki/Cres "Cres") and [Krk](/wiki/Krk "Krk"),[[157]](#cite_note-CIA-162) each of them having an area of around 405 square kilometres (156 square miles).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/Dugi_otok_island.jpg/250px-Dugi_otok_island.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dugi_otok_island.jpg)

[Telašćica Nature Park](/wiki/Tela%C5%A1%C4%87ica_Nature_Park "Telašćica Nature Park")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/20230429.Donau.zwischen_km_1255_und_1333.-032.1.jpg/250px-20230429.Donau.zwischen_km_1255_und_1333.-032.1.jpg)](/wiki/File:20230429.Donau.zwischen_km_1255_und_1333.-032.1.jpg)

[Tourist cruise](/wiki/River_cruise "River cruise") on the [Danube](/wiki/Danube "Danube") river, eastern [Slavonia](/wiki/Slavonia "Slavonia")

The hilly northern parts of [Hrvatsko Zagorje](/wiki/Hrvatsko_Zagorje "Hrvatsko Zagorje") and the flat plains of Slavonia in the east which is part of the [Pannonian Basin](/wiki/Pannonian_Basin "Pannonian Basin") are traversed by major rivers such as [Danube](/wiki/Danube "Danube"), [Drava](/wiki/Drava "Drava"), [Kupa](/wiki/Kupa "Kupa"), and the [Sava](/wiki/Sava "Sava"). The Danube, Europe's second longest river, runs through the city of [Vukovar](/wiki/Vukovar "Vukovar") in the extreme east and forms part of the border with [Vojvodina](/wiki/Vojvodina "Vojvodina"). The central and southern regions near the Adriatic coastline and islands consist of low mountains and forested highlands. Natural resources found in quantities significant enough for production include oil, coal, bauxite, low-grade iron ore, calcium, gypsum, natural asphalt, silica, mica, clays, salt, and hydropower.[[157]](#cite_note-CIA-162) [Karst topography](/wiki/Karst_topography "Karst topography") makes up about half of Croatia and is especially prominent in the Dinaric Alps.[[158]](#cite_note-163) Croatia hosts [deep caves](/wiki/List_of_caves_in_Croatia "List of caves in Croatia"), 49 of which are deeper than 250 m (820.21 ft), 14 deeper than 500 m (1,640.42 ft) and three deeper than 1,000 m (3,280.84 ft). Croatia's most famous lakes are the [Plitvice lakes](/wiki/Plitvice_lakes "Plitvice lakes"), a system of 16 lakes with waterfalls connecting them over [dolomite](/wiki/Dolomite_(mineral) "Dolomite (mineral)") and [limestone](/wiki/Limestone "Limestone") cascades. The lakes are renowned for their distinctive colours, ranging from turquoise to mint green, grey or blue.[[159]](#cite_note-BBC-Plitvice-164)

### Climate

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Croatia_K%C3%B6ppen.svg/330px-Croatia_K%C3%B6ppen.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Croatia_K%C3%B6ppen.svg)

[Köppen-Geiger climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") map of Croatia

Most of Croatia has a moderately warm and rainy [continental climate](/wiki/Continental_climate "Continental climate") as defined by the [Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"). Mean monthly temperature ranges between −3 [°C](/wiki/Celsius "Celsius") (27 [°F](/wiki/Fahrenheit "Fahrenheit")) in January and 18 °C (64 °F) in July. The coldest parts of the country are [Lika](/wiki/Lika "Lika") and [Gorski Kotar](/wiki/Gorski_Kotar "Gorski Kotar") featuring a snowy, forested climate at elevations above 1,200 metres (3,900 feet). The warmest areas are at the Adriatic coast and especially in its immediate hinterland characterised by [Mediterranean climate](/wiki/Mediterranean_climate "Mediterranean climate"), as the sea moderates temperature highs. Consequently, temperature peaks are more pronounced in continental areas.

The lowest temperature of −35.5 °C (−31.9 °F) was recorded on 3 February 1919 in [Čakovec](/wiki/%C4%8Cakovec "Čakovec"),[[160]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTE2018_Statistical_Yearbook_of_the_Republic_of_Croatia40-165) and the highest temperature of 42.8 °C (109.0 °F) was recorded on 4 August 1981 in [Ploče](/wiki/Plo%C4%8De "Ploče").[[161]](#cite_note-record_temp-166)

Mean annual precipitation ranges between 600 millimetres (24 inches) and 3,500 millimetres (140 inches) depending on geographic region and climate type. The least precipitation is recorded in the outer islands ([Biševo](/wiki/Bi%C5%A1evo "Biševo"), [Lastovo](/wiki/Lastovo "Lastovo"), [Svetac](/wiki/Svetac "Svetac"), [Vis](/wiki/Vis_(island) "Vis (island)")) and the eastern parts of Slavonia. However, in the latter case, rain occurs mostly during the [growing season](/wiki/Growing_season "Growing season"). The maximum precipitation levels are observed in the Dinaric Alps, in the Gorski Kotar peaks of [Risnjak](/wiki/Risnjak "Risnjak") and [Snježnik](/wiki/Snje%C5%BEnik "Snježnik").[[160]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTE2018_Statistical_Yearbook_of_the_Republic_of_Croatia40-165)

Prevailing winds in the interior are light to moderate northeast or southwest, and in the coastal area, prevailing winds are determined by local features. Higher wind velocities are more often recorded in cooler months along the coast, generally as the cool northeasterly [bura](/wiki/Bora_(wind) "Bora (wind)") or less frequently as the warm southerly [jugo](/wiki/Jugo "Jugo"). The sunniest parts are the outer islands, Hvar and Korčula, where more than 2700 hours of sunshine are recorded per year, followed by the middle and southern Adriatic Sea area in general, and northern Adriatic coast, all with more than 2000 hours of sunshine per year.[[162]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTE2018_Statistical_Yearbook_of_the_Republic_of_Croatia41-167)

### Biodiversity

Main article: [Protected areas of Croatia](/wiki/Protected_areas_of_Croatia "Protected areas of Croatia")

Further information: [Register of Protected Natural Values of Croatia](/wiki/Register_of_Protected_Natural_Values_of_Croatia "Register of Protected Natural Values of Croatia") and [Natural and Cultural Heritage of Croatia](/wiki/Natural_and_Cultural_Heritage_of_Croatia "Natural and Cultural Heritage of Croatia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/Waterfalls_at_Plitvica_stream_in_Plitvice_Lakes_National_Park%2C_Croatia_%2848607294088%29.jpg/250px-Waterfalls_at_Plitvica_stream_in_Plitvice_Lakes_National_Park%2C_Croatia_%2848607294088%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Waterfalls_at_Plitvica_stream_in_Plitvice_Lakes_National_Park,_Croatia_(48607294088).jpg)

[Plitvice Lakes National Park](/wiki/Plitvice_Lakes_National_Park "Plitvice Lakes National Park")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/RRI-0036-1962_view_to_Motovun.jpg/250px-RRI-0036-1962_view_to_Motovun.jpg)](/wiki/File:RRI-0036-1962_view_to_Motovun.jpg)

Landscapes of [Motovun](/wiki/Motovun "Motovun") in [Istrian peninsula](/wiki/Istria_County "Istria County")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/Gale%C5%A1njak_Island_in_Croatia_%2817114069092%29.jpg/250px-Gale%C5%A1njak_Island_in_Croatia_%2817114069092%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gale%C5%A1njak_Island_in_Croatia_(17114069092).jpg)

[Galešnjak](/wiki/Gale%C5%A1njak "Galešnjak") island in a shape of a heart

Croatia can be subdivided into [ecoregions](/wiki/Ecoregion "Ecoregion") based on climate and geomorphology. The country is one of the richest in Europe in terms of biodiversity.[[163]](#cite_note-168)[[164]](#cite_note-169) Croatia has four types of biogeographical regions—the [Mediterranean](/wiki/Mediterranean_Biogeographic_Region "Mediterranean Biogeographic Region") along the coast and in its immediate hinterland, [Alpine](/wiki/Alpine_Biogeographic_Region "Alpine Biogeographic Region") in most of Lika and Gorski Kotar, [Pannonian](/wiki/Pannonian_Biogeographic_Region "Pannonian Biogeographic Region") along Drava and Danube, and [Continental](/wiki/Continental_Biogeographic_Region "Continental Biogeographic Region") in the remaining areas. The most significant are [karst](/wiki/Karst "Karst") habitats which include submerged karst, such as [Zrmanja](/wiki/Zrmanja "Zrmanja") and [Krka](/wiki/Krka_(Croatia) "Krka (Croatia)") canyons and tufa barriers, as well as underground habitats. The country contains three ecoregions: [Dinaric Mountains mixed forests](/wiki/Dinaric_Mountains_mixed_forests "Dinaric Mountains mixed forests"), [Pannonian mixed forests](/wiki/Pannonian_mixed_forests "Pannonian mixed forests"), and [Illyrian deciduous forests](/wiki/Illyrian_deciduous_forests "Illyrian deciduous forests").[[165]](#cite_note-DinersteinOlson2017-170)

The karst geology harbours approximately [7,000 caves and pits](/wiki/List_of_caves_in_Croatia "List of caves in Croatia"), some of which are the habitat of the only known aquatic cave [vertebrate](/wiki/Vertebrate "Vertebrate")—the [olm](/wiki/Olm "Olm"). Forests are abundant, covering 2,490,000 hectares (6,200,000 acres) or 44% of Croatian land area. Other habitat types include wetlands, grasslands, bogs, fens, scrub habitats, coastal and marine habitats.[[166]](#cite_note-MCult-Biodiversity-171)

In terms of [phytogeography](/wiki/Phytogeography "Phytogeography"), Croatia is a part of the [Boreal Kingdom](/wiki/Boreal_Kingdom "Boreal Kingdom") and is a part of Illyrian and Central European provinces of the [Circumboreal Region](/wiki/Circumboreal_Region "Circumboreal Region") and the Adriatic province of the [Mediterranean Region](/wiki/Mediterranean_Region "Mediterranean Region"). The [World Wide Fund for Nature](/wiki/World_Wide_Fund_for_Nature "World Wide Fund for Nature") divides Croatia between three ecoregions—Pannonian mixed forests, [Dinaric Mountains mixed forests](/wiki/Dinaric_Mountains_mixed_forests "Dinaric Mountains mixed forests") and [Illyrian deciduous forests](/wiki/Illyrian_deciduous_forests "Illyrian deciduous forests").[[167]](#cite_note-DU-Sustdev-172)

Croatia hosts 37,000 known plant and animal species, but their actual number is estimated to be between 50,000 and 100,000.[[166]](#cite_note-MCult-Biodiversity-171) More than a thousand species are endemic, especially in Velebit and Biokovo mountains, Adriatic islands and karst rivers. Legislation protects 1,131 species.[[166]](#cite_note-MCult-Biodiversity-171) The most serious threat is habitat loss and degradation. A further problem is presented by invasive alien species, especially *[Caulerpa taxifolia](/wiki/Caulerpa_taxifolia "Caulerpa taxifolia")* algae.

Invasive algae are regularly monitored and removed to protect [benthic habitat](/wiki/Benthic_habitat "Benthic habitat"). Indigenous cultivated plant strains and domesticated animal breeds are numerous. They include five breeds of horses, five of cattle, eight of sheep, two of pigs, and one poultry. Indigenous breeds include nine that are endangered or critically endangered.[[166]](#cite_note-MCult-Biodiversity-171) Croatia has 444 [protected areas](/wiki/Protected_areas_of_Croatia "Protected areas of Croatia"), encompassing 9% of the country. Those include eight [national parks](/wiki/List_of_national_parks_of_Croatia "List of national parks of Croatia"), two strict reserves, and ten [nature parks](/wiki/Nature_park "Nature park"). The most famous protected area and the oldest [national park](/wiki/National_park "National park") in Croatia is [Plitvice Lakes National Park](/wiki/Plitvice_Lakes_National_Park "Plitvice Lakes National Park"), a [UNESCO World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Sites_in_Croatia "World Heritage Sites in Croatia"). Velebit Nature Park is a part of the UNESCO [Man and the Biosphere Programme](/wiki/Man_and_the_Biosphere_Programme "Man and the Biosphere Programme"). The strict and special reserves, as well as the national and nature parks, are managed and protected by the central government, while other protected areas are managed by counties. In 2005, the National Ecological Network was set up, as the first step in the preparation of the EU accession and joining of the [Natura 2000](/wiki/Natura_2000 "Natura 2000") network.[[166]](#cite_note-MCult-Biodiversity-171)

## Governance

Further information: [Politics of Croatia](/wiki/Politics_of_Croatia "Politics of Croatia") and [Human rights in Croatia](/wiki/Human_rights_in_Croatia "Human rights in Croatia")
