The Republic of Croatia is a unitary, constitutional state using a [parliamentary system](/wiki/Parliamentary_system "Parliamentary system"). Government powers in Croatia are legislative, executive, and judiciary powers.[[168]](#cite_note-VRH-PoliticalStructure-173)
The [president of the republic](/wiki/President_of_Croatia "President of Croatia") ([Croatian](/wiki/Croatian_language "Croatian language"): *Predsjednik Republike*) is the [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state"), directly elected to a five-year term and is limited by the Constitution to two terms. In addition to serving as [commander in chief](/wiki/Commander_in_chief "Commander in chief") of the armed forces, the president has the procedural duty of appointing the prime minister with the parliament and has some influence on foreign policy.[[168]](#cite_note-VRH-PoliticalStructure-173)

The [Government](/wiki/Croatian_Government "Croatian Government") is headed by the [prime minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Croatia "Prime Minister of Croatia"), who has four deputy prime ministers and 16 ministers in charge of particular sectors.[[169]](#cite_note-Govt-Ministers-174) As the [executive branch](/wiki/Executive_(government) "Executive (government)"), it is responsible for proposing legislation and a budget, enforcing the laws, and guiding foreign and internal policies. The Government is seated at [Banski dvori](/wiki/Banski_dvori "Banski dvori") in Zagreb.[[168]](#cite_note-VRH-PoliticalStructure-173)

### Law and judicial system

Further information: [Law of Croatia](/wiki/Law_of_Croatia "Law of Croatia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/Sabor-pro%C4%8Delje.jpg/250px-Sabor-pro%C4%8Delje.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sabor-pro%C4%8Delje.jpg)

[Croatian Parliament](/wiki/Croatian_Parliament "Croatian Parliament") (Sabor) in Zagreb

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Banski_dvori_%28kolovoz_2021%29.jpg/250px-Banski_dvori_%28kolovoz_2021%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Banski_dvori_(kolovoz_2021).jpg)

[Banski dvori](/wiki/Banski_dvori "Banski dvori") in Zagreb, seat of the [Government of Croatia](/wiki/Government_of_Croatia "Government of Croatia")

A [unicameral](/wiki/Unicameral "Unicameral") parliament (**Sabor**) holds [legislative power](/wiki/Legislative_power "Legislative power"). The number of Sabor members can vary from 100 to 160. They are elected by popular vote to serve four-year terms. Legislative sessions take place from 15 January to 15 July, and from 15 September to 15 December annually.[[170]](#cite_note-Sabor-about-175) The two largest [political parties in Croatia](/wiki/Political_parties_in_Croatia "Political parties in Croatia") are the [Croatian Democratic Union](/wiki/Croatian_Democratic_Union "Croatian Democratic Union") and the [Social Democratic Party of Croatia](/wiki/Social_Democratic_Party_of_Croatia "Social Democratic Party of Croatia").[[171]](#cite_note-Sabor-MPs-176)

Croatia has a [civil law](/wiki/Civil_law_(legal_system) "Civil law (legal system)") legal system in which law arises primarily from written statutes, with judges serving as implementers and not creators of law. Its development was largely influenced by German and Austrian legal systems. Croatian law is divided into two principal areas—[private](/wiki/Private_law "Private law") and [public law](/wiki/Public_law "Public law"). Before [EU accession negotiations](/wiki/Accession_of_Croatia_to_the_European_Union "Accession of Croatia to the European Union") were completed, Croatian legislation had been fully harmonised with the [Community acquis](/wiki/Community_acquis "Community acquis").[[172]](#cite_note-DELHRV-overview-177)

The main national courts are the [Constitutional Court](/wiki/Constitutional_Court_of_Croatia "Constitutional Court of Croatia"), which oversees violations of the Constitution, and the [Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Croatia "Supreme Court of Croatia"), which is the highest court of appeal. Administrative, Commercial, [County](/wiki/County_Court "County Court"), Misdemeanor, and Municipal courts handle cases in their respective domains.[[173]](#cite_note-VSRH-about-178) Cases falling within judicial jurisdiction are in the first instance decided by a single professional judge, while appeals are deliberated in mixed tribunals of professional judges. Lay magistrates also participate in trials.[[174]](#cite_note-179) The [State's Attorney Office](/wiki/State%27s_Attorney_Office_of_the_Republic_of_Croatia "State's Attorney Office of the Republic of Croatia") is the judicial body constituted of public prosecutors empowered to instigate prosecution of perpetrators of offences.[[175]](#cite_note-180)

Law enforcement agencies are organised under the authority of the [Ministry of the Interior](/wiki/Ministry_of_the_Interior_(Croatia) "Ministry of the Interior (Croatia)") which consist primarily of the national police force. Croatia's security service is the [Security and Intelligence Agency](/wiki/Security_and_Intelligence_Agency_(Croatia) "Security and Intelligence Agency (Croatia)") (SOA).[[176]](#cite_note-181)[[177]](#cite_note-182)

### Foreign relations

Main articles: [Foreign relations of Croatia](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_Croatia "Foreign relations of Croatia") and [Croatia and the euro](/wiki/Croatia_and_the_euro "Croatia and the euro")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/Official_Photo_NATO_-_OTAN_Summit_2025_on_June_25%2C_2025_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Official_Photo_NATO_-_OTAN_Summit_2025_on_June_25%2C_2025_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Official_Photo_NATO_-_OTAN_Summit_2025_on_June_25,_2025_(cropped).jpg)

President [Zoran Milanović](/wiki/Zoran_Milanovi%C4%87 "Zoran Milanović") with other Western leaders at the [2025 NATO Summit](/wiki/2025_The_Hague_NATO_summit "2025 The Hague NATO summit") in [The Hague](/wiki/The_Hague "The Hague"), [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands") attending discussions on security and defense cooperation within [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO").

Croatia has established diplomatic relations with 194 countries.[[178]](#cite_note-183) supporting 57 embassies, 30 consulates and eight permanent diplomatic missions. 56 foreign embassies and 67 [consulates](/wiki/Consul_(representative) "Consul (representative)") operate in the country in addition to offices of international organisations such as the [European Bank for Reconstruction and Development](/wiki/European_Bank_for_Reconstruction_and_Development "European Bank for Reconstruction and Development") (EBRD), [International Organization for Migration](/wiki/International_Organization_for_Migration "International Organization for Migration") (IOM), [Organization for Security and Co-operation in Europe](/wiki/Organization_for_Security_and_Co-operation_in_Europe "Organization for Security and Co-operation in Europe") (OSCE), [World Bank](/wiki/World_Bank "World Bank"), [World Health Organization](/wiki/World_Health_Organization "World Health Organization") (WHO), [International Criminal Tribunal for the former Yugoslavia](/wiki/International_Criminal_Tribunal_for_the_former_Yugoslavia "International Criminal Tribunal for the former Yugoslavia") (ICTY), [United Nations Development Programme](/wiki/United_Nations_Development_Programme "United Nations Development Programme") (UNDP), [United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees](/wiki/United_Nations_High_Commissioner_for_Refugees "United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees") (UNHCR), and [UNICEF](/wiki/UNICEF "UNICEF").[[179]](#cite_note-184)

As of 2019, the Croatian [Ministry of Foreign Affairs and European Integration](/wiki/Ministry_of_Foreign_Affairs_and_European_Integration_(Croatia) "Ministry of Foreign Affairs and European Integration (Croatia)") employed 1,381[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] personnel and expended 765.295 million kunas (€101.17 million).[[180]](#cite_note-185) Stated aims of Croatian foreign policy include enhancing relations with neighbouring countries, developing international co-operation and promotion of the Croatian economy and Croatia itself.[[181]](#cite_note-MVPEI-Goals-186)

Croatia is a member of the European Union. As of 2021, Croatia had unsolved border issues with Bosnia and Herzegovina, Montenegro, Serbia, and Slovenia.[[182]](#cite_note-Border_issues-187) Croatia is a member of NATO.[[183]](#cite_note-NYTimes-Bush-Zagreb-188)[[184]](#cite_note-BBC-Croatia-NATO-189) On 1 January 2023, Croatia simultaneously joined both the [Schengen Area](/wiki/Schengen_Area "Schengen Area") and the [Eurozone](/wiki/Eurozone "Eurozone"),[[185]](#cite_note-BBC-Croatia-EU-190) having previously joined the ERM II on 10 July 2020.

### Croatian diaspora

Main article: [Croatian diaspora](/wiki/Croatian_diaspora "Croatian diaspora")

The Croatian diaspora consists of communities of ethnic [Croats](/wiki/Croats "Croats") and Croatian citizens living outside Croatia. Croatia maintains intensive contacts with Croatian communities abroad (e.g., administrative and financial support of cultural, sports activities, and economic initiatives). Croatia actively maintain foreign relations to strengthen and guarantee the rights of the Croatian minority in various host countries.[[186]](#cite_note-191)[[187]](#cite_note-192)[[188]](#cite_note-193)

### Military

Main article: [Armed Forces of Croatia](/wiki/Armed_Forces_of_Croatia "Armed Forces of Croatia")

The Croatian Armed Forces (CAF) consist of the [Air Force](/wiki/Croatian_Air_Force_and_Air_Defence "Croatian Air Force and Air Defence"), [Army](/wiki/Croatian_Army "Croatian Army"), and [Navy](/wiki/Croatian_Navy "Croatian Navy") branches in addition to the Education and Training Command and Support Command. The CAF is headed by the [General Staff](/wiki/General_Staff_of_the_Armed_Forces_of_the_Republic_of_Croatia "General Staff of the Armed Forces of the Republic of Croatia"), which reports to the [defence minister](/wiki/Ministry_of_Defence_(Croatia) "Ministry of Defence (Croatia)"), who in turn reports to the president. According to the constitution, the president is the [commander-in-chief](/wiki/Commander-in-chief "Commander-in-chief") of the armed forces. In case of immediate threat during wartime, he issues orders directly to the General Staff.[[189]](#cite_note-CAFstructure-194)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/Najve%C4%87i_vojni_mimohod_u_povijesti_Hrvatske_zavr%C5%A1io_preletom_devet_Rafalea_XXV.jpg/250px-Najve%C4%87i_vojni_mimohod_u_povijesti_Hrvatske_zavr%C5%A1io_preletom_devet_Rafalea_XXV.jpg)](/wiki/File:Najve%C4%87i_vojni_mimohod_u_povijesti_Hrvatske_zavr%C5%A1io_preletom_devet_Rafalea_XXV.jpg)

[Croatian Navy](/wiki/Croatian_Navy "Croatian Navy").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Uvje%C5%BEbavanje_mehaniziranih_e%C5%A1alona_Hrvatske_vojske_za_sve%C4%8Dani_vojni_mimohod_povodom_30._obljetnice_VRO_Oluja_II.jpg/250px-Uvje%C5%BEbavanje_mehaniziranih_e%C5%A1alona_Hrvatske_vojske_za_sve%C4%8Dani_vojni_mimohod_povodom_30._obljetnice_VRO_Oluja_II.jpg)](/wiki/File:Uvje%C5%BEbavanje_mehaniziranih_e%C5%A1alona_Hrvatske_vojske_za_sve%C4%8Dani_vojni_mimohod_povodom_30._obljetnice_VRO_Oluja_II.jpg)

[Armed Forces of Croatia](/wiki/Armed_Forces_of_Croatia "Armed Forces of Croatia").

Following the 1991–95 war, defence spending and CAF size began a constant decline. As of 2019[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Croatia&action=edit), military spending was an estimated 1.68% of the country's GDP, 67th globally.[[190]](#cite_note-195) In 2005 the budget fell below the NATO-required 2% of GDP, down from the record high of 11.1% in 1994.[[191]](#cite_note-sipri-196) Traditionally relying on conscripts, the CAF went through a period of reforms focused on downsizing, restructuring and [professionalisation](/wiki/Professionalisation "Professionalisation") in the years before [accession to NATO](/wiki/Accession_of_Croatia_to_NATO "Accession of Croatia to NATO") in April 2009. According to a presidential decree issued in 2006, the CAF employed around 18,100 active duty military personnel, 3,000 civilians and 2,000 voluntary conscripts between 18 and 30 years old in peacetime.[[189]](#cite_note-CAFstructure-194)

Until 2008 military service was obligatory for men at age 18 and conscripts served six-month tours of duty, reduced in 2001 from the earlier scheme of nine months. Conscientious objectors could instead opt for eight months of civilian service.[[192]](#cite_note-vojnirokkraci-197) Compulsory conscription was abolished in January 2008,[[157]](#cite_note-CIA-162) but is set to be reintroduced in January 2025 with two months of active duty. The decision was influenced by the rising tensions in Europe and the region, following the [2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/2022_Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine").[[193]](#cite_note-198)[[194]](#cite_note-199)
As of May 2019[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Croatia&action=edit), the Croatian military had 72 members stationed in foreign countries as part of United Nations-led international peacekeeping forces.[[195]](#cite_note-200) As of 2019[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Croatia&action=edit), 323 troops served the NATO-led [ISAF](/wiki/International_Security_Assistance_Force "International Security Assistance Force") force in Afghanistan. Another 156 served with [KFOR](/wiki/Kosovo_Force "Kosovo Force") in Kosovo.[[196]](#cite_note-201)[[197]](#cite_note-202)

Croatia has a [military-industrial](/wiki/Military_industry "Military industry") sector that exported around 493 million kunas (€65,176 million) worth of military equipment in 2020.[[198]](#cite_note-203) Croatian-made weapons and vehicles used by CAF include the standard sidearm [HS2000](/wiki/HS2000 "HS2000") manufactured by [HS Produkt](/wiki/HS_Produkt "HS Produkt") and the [M-84D](/wiki/M-84D "M-84D") battle tank designed by the [Đuro Đaković factory](/wiki/%C4%90uro_%C4%90akovi%C4%87_factory "Đuro Đaković factory"). Uniforms and helmets worn by CAF soldiers are locally produced and marketed to other countries.[[199]](#cite_note-vojna_ind-204)

According to the 2024 [Global Peace Index](/wiki/Global_Peace_Index "Global Peace Index"), Croatia is the 15th most peaceful country in the world.[[200]](#cite_note-205)

Counties of Croatia

Croatia was first divided into counties in the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages").[[201]](#cite_note-Medieval-Counties-206) The divisions changed over time to reflect losses of territory to Ottoman conquest and subsequent liberation of the same territory, changes of the political status of Dalmatia, Dubrovnik, and [Istria](/wiki/Istria "Istria"). The traditional division of the country into counties was abolished in the 1920s when the Kingdom of Serbs, Croats and Slovenes and the subsequent Kingdom of Yugoslavia introduced [oblasts](/wiki/Oblast "Oblast") and [banovinas](/wiki/Subdivisions_of_the_Kingdom_of_Yugoslavia "Subdivisions of the Kingdom of Yugoslavia") respectively.[[202]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFrucht2005429-207)

Communist-ruled Croatia, as a constituent part of post-World War II Yugoslavia, abolished earlier divisions and introduced municipalities, subdividing Croatia into approximately one hundred municipalities. Counties were reintroduced in 1992 legislation, significantly altered in terms of territory relative to the pre-1920s subdivisions. In 1918, the [Transleithanian](/wiki/Transleithania "Transleithania") part was divided into eight counties with their seats in [Bjelovar](/wiki/Bjelovar "Bjelovar"), [Gospić](/wiki/Gospi%C4%87 "Gospić"), [Ogulin](/wiki/Ogulin "Ogulin"), [Osijek](/wiki/Osijek "Osijek"), [Požega](/wiki/Po%C5%BEega,_Croatia "Požega, Croatia"), [Varaždin](/wiki/Vara%C5%BEdin "Varaždin"), Vukovar, and Zagreb.[[203]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBiondich200011-208)[[204]](#cite_note-CountiesAct1992-209)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Castle_of_Varazdin_%2817%29.jpg/250px-Castle_of_Varazdin_%2817%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Castle_of_Varazdin_(17).jpg)

[Varaždin](/wiki/Vara%C5%BEdin "Varaždin"), capital of Croatia between 1767 and 1776, is the seat of [Varaždin county](/wiki/Vara%C5%BEdin_county "Varaždin county"); Pictured: Old Town fortress, one of 15 Croatia's sites inscribed on the [UNESCO World Heritage tentative list](/wiki/List_of_World_Heritage_Sites_in_Croatia#Tentative_list "List of World Heritage Sites in Croatia")

As of 1992, Croatia is divided into [20 counties](/wiki/Counties_of_Croatia "Counties of Croatia") and the [capital city of Zagreb](/wiki/Capital_city_of_Zagreb "Capital city of Zagreb"), the latter having the dual authority and legal status of a county and a city. County borders changed in some instances, last revised in 2006. The counties subdivide into [127 cities](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Croatia "List of cities in Croatia") and [429 municipalities](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Croatia "Municipalities of Croatia").[[205]](#cite_note-CountiesAct2006-210) [Nomenclature of Territorial Units for Statistics](/wiki/Nomenclature_of_Territorial_Units_for_Statistics "Nomenclature of Territorial Units for Statistics") (NUTS) [division](/wiki/Division_of_Croatia "Division of Croatia") is performed in several tiers. NUTS 1 level considers the entire country in a single unit; three NUTS 2 regions come below that. Those are Northwest Croatia, Central and Eastern (Pannonian) Croatia, and Adriatic Croatia. The latter encompasses the counties along the Adriatic coast. Northwest Croatia includes Koprivnica-Križevci, Krapina-Zagorje, Međimurje, Varaždin, the city of Zagreb, and Zagreb counties and the Central and Eastern (Pannonian) Croatia includes the remaining areas—Bjelovar-Bilogora, Brod-Posavina, Karlovac, Osijek-Baranja, Požega-Slavonia, Sisak-Moslavina, Virovitica-Podravina, and Vukovar-Syrmia counties. Individual counties and the city of Zagreb also represent NUTS 3 level subdivision units in Croatia. The NUTS [local administrative unit](/wiki/Administrative_division "Administrative division") (LAU) divisions are two-tiered. LAU 1 divisions match the counties and the city of Zagreb in effect making those the same as NUTS 3 units, while LAU 2 subdivisions correspond to cities and municipalities.[[206]](#cite_note-211)

## Economy

Main article: [Economy of Croatia](/wiki/Economy_of_Croatia "Economy of Croatia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/Rimac_Nevera.jpg/250px-Rimac_Nevera.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rimac_Nevera.jpg)

[Rimac Automobili](/wiki/Rimac_Automobili "Rimac Automobili") were designed and made in Croatia

Croatia's economy qualifies as [high-income](/wiki/High-income "High-income") and [developed](/wiki/Developed_country "Developed country").[[207]](#cite_note-World_Bank_Country_Classification-212) [International Monetary Fund](/wiki/International_Monetary_Fund "International Monetary Fund") data puts the Croatian [nominal GDP](/wiki/Nominal_GDP "Nominal GDP") at $103.90 Billion for 2025, or $26,960 per capita.[[208]](#cite_note-IMF-213) [Purchasing power parity](/wiki/Purchasing_power_parity "Purchasing power parity") (PPP) GDP is at 198.31 Billion, or $51,450 per capita.[[208]](#cite_note-IMF-213) According to [Eurostat](/wiki/Eurostat "Eurostat"), Croatian GDP per capita in PPS stood at 78% of the EU average in 2024, with real GDP growth for the year being 2.8%.[[209]](#cite_note-214)[[210]](#cite_note-215) The average net salary of a Croatian worker in May 2025 was €1,451 per month, the average gross salary roughly €2,019 per month.[[211]](#cite_note-Web_|_Državni_zavod_za_statistiku-216) The unemployment rate is at 4.2% in October 2025, down from 7.2% in July 2019 and 9.6% in December 2018.[[211]](#cite_note-Web_|_Državni_zavod_za_statistiku-216) The unemployment rate between 1996 and 2018 averaged 17.38%, reaching an all-time high of 23.60% in January 2002 and a record low of 8.40% in September 2018.[[212]](#cite_note-217) In 2017, economic output was dominated by the [service sector](/wiki/Service_sector "Service sector") — accounting for 70.1% of GDP – followed by the [industrial sector](/wiki/Industrial_sector "Industrial sector") with 26.2% and agriculture accounting for 3.7%.[[213]](#cite_note-cia-218)

According to 2017 data, 1.9% of the workforce were employed in agriculture, 27.3% by industry and 70.8% in services.[[213]](#cite_note-cia-218) Shipbuilding, food processing, pharmaceuticals, information technology, biochemical, and timber industry dominate the industrial sector. In 2018, Croatian exports were valued at 108 Billion kunas (€14.61 Billion) with 176 Billion kunas (€23.82 Billion) worth of imports. Croatia's largest [trading partner](/wiki/International_trade "International trade") was the rest of the European Union, led by Germany, Italy, and Slovenia.[[214]](#cite_note-219) According to Eurostat, Croatia has the highest quantity of water resources per capita in the EU (30,000 m3).[[215]](#cite_note-220)

As a result of the war, economic infrastructure sustained massive damage, particularly the tourism industry. From 1989 to 1993, the GDP fell 40.5%. The Croatian state still controls significant economic sectors, with government expenditures accounting for 40% of GDP.[[216]](#cite_note-DOS-221) A particular concern is a backlogged judiciary system, with inefficient [public administration](/wiki/Public_administration "Public administration") and corruption, upending land ownership. In the 2022 [Corruption Perceptions Index](/wiki/Corruption_Perceptions_Index "Corruption Perceptions Index"), published by [Transparency International](/wiki/Transparency_International "Transparency International"), the country ranked 57th.[[217]](#cite_note-ti_2022-222) At the end of June 2020, the national debt stood at 85.3% of GDP.[[218]](#cite_note-223)

### Tourism

Main articles: [Tourism in Croatia](/wiki/Tourism_in_Croatia "Tourism in Croatia") and [Croatian National Tourist Board](/wiki/Croatian_National_Tourist_Board "Croatian National Tourist Board")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/Bokar_Fortress_and_the_Old_Town_of_Dubrovnik%2C_Croatia_%2848613181692%29.jpg/250px-Bokar_Fortress_and_the_Old_Town_of_Dubrovnik%2C_Croatia_%2848613181692%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bokar_Fortress_and_the_Old_Town_of_Dubrovnik,_Croatia_(48613181692).jpg)

[Dubrovnik](/wiki/Dubrovnik "Dubrovnik") is Croatia's most visited and most popular destination.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/Aerial_view_of_Zlatni_rat_%28Golden_Horn%29_Beach_in_Bol%2C_Croatia_%2848608813081%29.jpg/250px-Aerial_view_of_Zlatni_rat_%28Golden_Horn%29_Beach_in_Bol%2C_Croatia_%2848608813081%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Aerial_view_of_Zlatni_rat_(Golden_Horn)_Beach_in_Bol,_Croatia_(48608813081).jpg)

[Zlatni Rat](/wiki/Zlatni_Rat "Zlatni Rat") beach on the Island of [Brač](/wiki/Bra%C4%8D "Brač") is one of the foremost spots of [tourism in Croatia](/wiki/Tourism_in_Croatia "Tourism in Croatia").

Tourism dominates the Croatian service sector and accounts for up to 20% of GDP. Tourism income for 2019 was estimated to be €10.5 billion.[[219]](#cite_note-224) Its positive effects are felt throughout the economy, increasing retail business, and increasing seasonal employment. The industry is counted as an export business because foreign visitor spending significantly reduces the country's trade imbalance.[[220]](#cite_note-225)

The tourist industry has rapidly grown, recording a sharp rise in tourist numbers since independence, attracting more than 17 million visitors each year (as of 2017[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Croatia&action=edit)).[[221]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTE2018_Statistical_Yearbook_of_the_Republic_of_Croatia425-226) Germany, Slovenia, Austria, Italy, United Kingdom, Czechia, Poland, Hungary, France, Netherlands, Slovakia and Croatia itself provide the most visitors.[[222]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTE2018_Statistical_Yearbook_of_the_Republic_of_Croatia427-227) Tourist stays averaged 4.7 days in 2019.[[223]](#cite_note-228)

Much of the tourist industry is concentrated along the coast. [Opatija](/wiki/Opatija "Opatija") was the first holiday resort. It first became popular in the middle of the 19th century. By the 1890s, it had become one of the largest European health resorts.[[224]](#cite_note-229) Resorts sprang up along the coast and islands, offering services catering to [mass tourism](/wiki/Mass_tourism "Mass tourism") and various niche markets. The most significant are [nautical tourism](/wiki/Nautical_tourism "Nautical tourism"), supported by marinas with more than 16 thousand berths, [cultural tourism](/wiki/Cultural_tourism "Cultural tourism") relying on the appeal of medieval coastal cities and cultural events taking place during the summer. Inland areas offer [agrotourism](/wiki/Agrotourism "Agrotourism"), [mountain resorts](/wiki/Mountain_resort "Mountain resort"), and [spas](/wiki/Spa "Spa"). [Zagreb](/wiki/Zagreb "Zagreb") is a significant destination, rivalling major coastal cities and resorts.[[225]](#cite_note-230)

Croatia has unpolluted marine areas with nature reserves and 116 [Blue Flag beaches](/wiki/Blue_Flag_beach "Blue Flag beach").[[226]](#cite_note-231) Croatia was ranked first in Europe for swimming water quality in 2022 by the [European Environment Agency](/wiki/European_Environment_Agency "European Environment Agency").[[227]](#cite_note-232)

Croatia ranked as the 23rd-most popular tourist destination in the world according to the [World Tourism Organization](/wiki/World_Tourism_Organization "World Tourism Organization") in 2019.[[228]](#cite_note-UNWTO-233) About 15% of these visitors,[*[which?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Avoid_weasel_words "Wikipedia:Avoid weasel words")*][*[quantify](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] or over one million per year, participate in [naturism](/wiki/Naturism "Naturism"), for which Croatia is famous. It was the first European country to develop commercial naturist resorts.[[229]](#cite_note-234) In 2023, luggage storage company Bounce gave Croatia the highest solo travel index in the world (7.58),[[230]](#cite_note-235) while a joint [Pinterest](/wiki/Pinterest "Pinterest") and [Zola](/wiki/Zola_(company) "Zola (company)") wedding trends report from 2023 put Croatia among the most popular honeymoon destinations.[[231]](#cite_note-236)

### Infrastructure

See also: [Transport in Croatia](/wiki/Transport_in_Croatia "Transport in Croatia"), [Energy in Croatia](/wiki/Energy_in_Croatia "Energy in Croatia"), and [Tallest buildings in Croatia](/wiki/Tallest_buildings_in_Croatia "Tallest buildings in Croatia")

#### Motorways

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/P067539-946135_Pelje%C5%A1ac_bridge.jpg/250px-P067539-946135_Pelje%C5%A1ac_bridge.jpg)](/wiki/File:P067539-946135_Pelje%C5%A1ac_bridge.jpg)

[Pelješac Bridge](/wiki/Pelje%C5%A1ac_Bridge "Pelješac Bridge") connects the peninsula of [Pelješac](/wiki/Pelje%C5%A1ac "Pelješac") and through it the southernmost part, including [Dubrovnik](/wiki/Dubrovnik "Dubrovnik"), with the [Croatian mainland](/wiki/Croatia_proper "Croatia proper").

The [motorway network](/wiki/Motorways_in_Croatia "Motorways in Croatia") was largely built in the late 1990s and the 2000s. As of December 2020, Croatia had completed 1,313.8 kilometres (816.4 miles) of motorways, connecting Zagreb to other regions and following various [European routes](/wiki/International_E-road_network "International E-road network") and four [Pan-European corridors](/wiki/Pan-European_corridors "Pan-European corridors").[[232]](#cite_note-Helsinki-1997-237)[[233]](#cite_note-NN-Motorways2009-238)[[234]](#cite_note-239) The busiest motorways are the [A1](/wiki/A1_(Croatia) "A1 (Croatia)"), connecting Zagreb to Split and the [A3](/wiki/A3_(Croatia) "A3 (Croatia)"), passing east to west through northwest Croatia and Slavonia.[[235]](#cite_note-HC-promet-240)

A widespread network of [state roads in Croatia](/wiki/State_roads_in_Croatia "State roads in Croatia") acts as motorway [feeder roads](/wiki/Feeder_line_(network) "Feeder line (network)") while connecting major settlements. The high quality and safety levels of the Croatian motorway network were tested and confirmed by EuroTAP and EuroTest programmes.[[236]](#cite_note-241)[[237]](#cite_note-242)

The construction of 2.4-kilometre-long [Pelješac Bridge](/wiki/Pelje%C5%A1ac_Bridge "Pelješac Bridge") connected the two-halves of Dubrovnik-Neretva County and shortened the route to the [Pelješac](/wiki/Pelje%C5%A1ac "Pelješac") peninsula and the islands of [Korčula](/wiki/Kor%C4%8Dula "Korčula") and [Lastovo](/wiki/Lastovo "Lastovo") by more than 32 km. The construction of the bridge started in July 2018 after Croatian road operator [Hrvatske ceste](/wiki/Hrvatske_ceste "Hrvatske ceste") (HC) signed a 2.08 billion [kuna](/wiki/Croatian_kuna "Croatian kuna") deal for the works with a Chinese consortium led by [China Road and Bridge Corporation](/wiki/China_Road_and_Bridge_Corporation "China Road and Bridge Corporation") (CRBC). The project was co-financed by the European Union with €357 million. The construction was completed in July 2022.[[238]](#cite_note-243)

#### Railways

Main article: [Rail transport in Croatia](/wiki/Rail_transport_in_Croatia "Rail transport in Croatia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/Rafalei_do%C4%8Dekali_viceprvake_svijeta.jpg/250px-Rafalei_do%C4%8Dekali_viceprvake_svijeta.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rafalei_do%C4%8Dekali_viceprvake_svijeta.jpg)

[Croatia Airlines](/wiki/Croatia_Airlines "Croatia Airlines") is the national airline of Croatia and was formed in 1989. Croatia Airlines aircraft escorted by a [Croatian Air Force](/wiki/Croatian_Air_Force "Croatian Air Force") [Dassault Rafale](/wiki/Dassault_Rafale "Dassault Rafale") aircraft

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/Zagreb_Glavni_kolodvor_1p_-_pruga_prema_zapadu.jpg/250px-Zagreb_Glavni_kolodvor_1p_-_pruga_prema_zapadu.jpg)](/wiki/File:Zagreb_Glavni_kolodvor_1p_-_pruga_prema_zapadu.jpg)

[HŽ series 6112](/wiki/H%C5%BD_series_6112 "HŽ series 6112") manufactured by the Croatian company [Končar Group](/wiki/Kon%C4%8Dar_Group "Končar Group"), operated by [Croatian Railways](/wiki/Croatian_Railways "Croatian Railways")

Croatia has an [extensive rail network](/wiki/List_of_railways_in_Croatia "List of railways in Croatia") spanning 2,604 kilometres (1,618 miles), including 984 kilometres (611 miles) of electrified railways and 254 kilometres (158 miles) of double track railways.[[239]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTE2018_Statistical_Yearbook_of_the_Republic_of_Croatia350-244) The most significant railways in Croatia are within the Pan-European transport corridors Vb and X connecting [Rijeka](/wiki/Rijeka "Rijeka") to [Budapest](/wiki/Budapest "Budapest") and Ljubljana to Belgrade, both via [Zagreb](/wiki/Zagreb "Zagreb").[[232]](#cite_note-Helsinki-1997-237) In 2024, the [European Investment Bank](/wiki/European_Investment_Bank "European Investment Bank") committed €400 million to support the revitalization of the railway system, focusing on green and digital transformations.[[240]](#cite_note-245)[[241]](#cite_note-www.koncar.hr-246) In July 2024, a significant agreement was signed for the acquisition of six [electro-diesel multiple units](/wiki/Electro-diesel_multiple_unit "Electro-diesel multiple unit") (EDMUs) to improve connectivity between [Split](/wiki/Split,_Croatia "Split, Croatia") and Zagreb. Valued at €57.3 million and financed through an EIB loan, this project is part of the broader initiative to modernize Croatia's railway infrastructure.[[241]](#cite_note-www.koncar.hr-246)

#### Aviation

[Airports in Croatia](/wiki/List_of_airports_in_Croatia "List of airports in Croatia") are located and serves [Dubrovnik](/wiki/Dubrovnik_Airport "Dubrovnik Airport"), [Osijek](/wiki/Osijek_Airport "Osijek Airport"), [Pula](/wiki/Pula_Airport "Pula Airport"), [Rijeka](/wiki/Rijeka_Airport "Rijeka Airport"), [Split](/wiki/Split_Airport "Split Airport"), [Zadar](/wiki/Zadar_Airport "Zadar Airport"), and [Zagreb](/wiki/Zagreb_International_Airport "Zagreb International Airport").[[242]](#cite_note-MMPI-Airports-247) The largest and busiest is [Franjo Tuđman Airport](/wiki/Zagreb_Airport "Zagreb Airport") in Zagreb, followed by [Saint Jerome Airport](/wiki/Split_Airport "Split Airport") in Split, [Ruđer Bošković Airport](/wiki/Dubrovnik_Airport "Dubrovnik Airport") in Dubrovnik and [Zadar Airport](/wiki/Zadar_Airport "Zadar Airport") in Zadar, with annual passenger numbers exceeding one million respectively.[[243]](#cite_note-248) [Croatian airports in terms of passenger arrivals](/wiki/List_of_the_busiest_airports_in_Croatia "List of the busiest airports in Croatia") recorded more than 10 million tourists in 2018, 11 million in 2019 and 2023, 13 million in 2024. Airports in Croatia welcomed more than 14 million passengers in 2025.[[244]](#cite_note-249) In July 2024, the airline took delivery of its first [Airbus A220](/wiki/Airbus_A220 "Airbus A220")-300 aircraft, marking the beginning of a transition to a more modern and efficient fleet.[[245]](#cite_note-www.airbus.com_2024-250)[[246]](#cite_note-aircraft.airbus.com_2024-251) This initiative includes the acquisition of 13 A220-300 and two [A220-100](/wiki/Airbus_A220 "Airbus A220") aircraft, aiming to enhance operational efficiency with 25% lower fuel consumption and reduced emissions.[[245]](#cite_note-www.airbus.com_2024-250)[[246]](#cite_note-aircraft.airbus.com_2024-251) As of 2024, [Croatia Airlines](/wiki/Croatia_Airlines "Croatia Airlines") expanded its international network by introducing direct flights from [Zagreb](/wiki/Zagreb "Zagreb") to [Tirana](/wiki/Tirana "Tirana"), Berlin, and [Stockholm](/wiki/Stockholm "Stockholm").[[247]](#cite_note-252) Additionally, the airline launched services connecting [Hamburg](/wiki/Hamburg "Hamburg") to Zagreb starting 1 July 2024.[[248]](#cite_note-253)

#### Ports

The busiest cargo seaport is the [Port of Rijeka](/wiki/Port_of_Rijeka "Port of Rijeka"). The busiest passenger ports are [Split](/wiki/Port_of_Split "Port of Split") and Zadar.[[249]](#cite_note-WB-Rijeka-254)[[250]](#cite_note-MMPI-RL-plan-255) Many minor ports serve ferries connecting numerous islands and coastal cities with ferry lines to several cities in Italy.[[251]](#cite_note-AOLPP-Rijeka-256) The largest [river port](/wiki/Inland_port "Inland port") is [port of Vukovar](/wiki/Port_of_Vukovar "Port of Vukovar"), located on the [Danube](/wiki/Danube "Danube") in [Vukovar](/wiki/Vukovar "Vukovar"), representing the nation's outlet to the Pan-European transport corridor VII.[[232]](#cite_note-Helsinki-1997-237)[[252]](#cite_note-MMPI-River-Navigation-257)

#### Energy

See also: [Energy in Croatia](/wiki/Energy_in_Croatia "Energy in Croatia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/Refinery_Industry_cargo_ship_%2852848038055%29.jpg/250px-Refinery_Industry_cargo_ship_%2852848038055%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Refinery_Industry_cargo_ship_(52848038055).jpg)

The [LNG Terminal Croatia](/wiki/LNG_Hrvatska "LNG Hrvatska") in [Omišalj](/wiki/Omi%C5%A1alj "Omišalj") on Krk Island (near [Rijeka](/wiki/Rijeka "Rijeka"))

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/Vjetropark_kod_Senja2.JPG/250px-Vjetropark_kod_Senja2.JPG)](/wiki/File:Vjetropark_kod_Senja2.JPG)

Wind farm near [Senj](/wiki/Senj "Senj"), [Lika-Senj County](/wiki/Lika-Senj_County "Lika-Senj County")

610 kilometres (380 miles) of crude oil pipelines serve Croatia, connecting the [Rijeka](/wiki/Rijeka "Rijeka") oil terminal with refineries in Rijeka and [Sisak](/wiki/Sisak "Sisak"), and several transhipment terminals.
The system has a capacity of 20 million tonnes per year.[[253]](#cite_note-JANAF-system-258) The natural gas transportation system comprises 2,113 kilometres (1,313 miles) of trunk and regional pipelines, and more than 300 associated structures, connecting production rigs, the Okoli natural gas storage facility, 27 end-users and 37 distribution systems.[[254]](#cite_note-Plinacro-system-259) Croatia also plays an important role in regional energy security. The floating [liquefied natural gas](/wiki/Liquefied_natural_gas "Liquefied natural gas") import terminal off [Krk](/wiki/Krk "Krk") island [LNG Hrvatska](/wiki/LNG_Hrvatska "LNG Hrvatska") commenced operations on 1 January 2021, positioning Croatia as a regional energy leader and contributing to diversification of Europe's energy supply.[[13]](#cite_note-lng-18)

In 2010, Croatian energy production covered 85% of nationwide natural gas and 19% of oil demand.[[255]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTE2010_Statistical_Yearbook_of_the_Republic_of_Croatia298–302-260) In 2016, Croatia's primary energy production involved natural gas (24.8%), hydropower (28.3%), crude oil (13.6%), fuelwood (27.6%), and heat pumps and other renewable energy sources (5.7%).[[256]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTE2018_Statistical_Yearbook_of_the_Republic_of_Croatia307-261) In 2017, net total electrical power production reached 11,543 GWh, while it imported 12,157 GWh or about 40% of its electric power energy needs.[[257]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTE2018_Statistical_Yearbook_of_the_Republic_of_Croatia304-262)

[Krško Nuclear Power Plant](/wiki/Kr%C5%A1ko_Nuclear_Power_Plant "Krško Nuclear Power Plant"), in [Slovenia](/wiki/Slovenia "Slovenia"), supplies a large part of Croatian imports. 50% is owned by [Hrvatska elektroprivreda](/wiki/Hrvatska_elektroprivreda "Hrvatska elektroprivreda"), providing 15% of Croatia's electricity.[[258]](#cite_note-EUB-NEK-263)

## Demographics

Main articles: [Demographics of Croatia](/wiki/Demographics_of_Croatia "Demographics of Croatia") and [Croats](/wiki/Croats "Croats")

See also: [List of cities and towns in Croatia](/wiki/List_of_cities_and_towns_in_Croatia "List of cities and towns in Croatia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/Croatia_ethnicities_2021.svg/500px-Croatia_ethnicities_2021.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Croatia_ethnicities_2021.svg)

With an estimated population of 3.87 million in 2024, Croatia [ranks 128th by population](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_population "List of countries by population") in the world.[[259]](#cite_note-264) Its 2018 [population density](/wiki/List_of_sovereign_states_and_dependent_territories_by_population_density "List of sovereign states and dependent territories by population density") was 72.9 inhabitants per square kilometre, making Croatia one of the more sparsely populated European countries.[[260]](#cite_note-who0-265) The overall [life expectancy in Croatia](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_life_expectancy "List of countries by life expectancy") at birth was 77.7 years in 2024.[[213]](#cite_note-cia-218)

The [total fertility rate](/wiki/Total_fertility_rate "Total fertility rate") of 1.46 children per mother, is one of the [lowest in the world](/wiki/List_of_sovereign_states_and_dependent_territories_by_fertility_rate "List of sovereign states and dependent territories by fertility rate"), far below the replacement rate of 2.1; it remains considerably below the high of 6.18 children rate in 1885.[[213]](#cite_note-cia-218)[[261]](#cite_note-266) Croatia's [death rate](/wiki/Death_rate "Death rate") has continuously exceeded its [birth rate](/wiki/Birth_rate "Birth rate") since 1998.[[262]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTE2018_Statistical_Yearbook_of_the_Republic_of_Croatia113-267) Croatia subsequently has one of the world's oldest populations, with an average age of 45.1 years.[[157]](#cite_note-CIA-162) The population rose steadily from 2.1 million in 1857 until 1991, when it peaked at 4.7 million, with the exceptions of censuses taken in 1921 and 1948, i.e., following the world wars.[[263]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTE2018_Statistical_Yearbook_of_the_Republic_of_Croatia107-268) The natural growth rate is negative[[157]](#cite_note-CIA-162) with the [demographic transition](/wiki/Demographic_transition "Demographic transition") completed in the 1970s.[[264]](#cite_note-269) In recent years, the Croatian government has been pressured to increase permit quotas for foreign workers, reaching an all-time high of 68.100 in 2019.[[265]](#cite_note-270) In accordance with its immigration policy, Croatia is trying to entice emigrants to return.[[266]](#cite_note-271) From 2008 to 2018, Croatia's population dropped by 10%.[[267]](#cite_note-272)

The population decrease was greater a result of war for independence. The war displaced large numbers of the population and emigration increased. In 1991, in predominantly occupied areas, more than 400,000 Croats were either removed from their homes by [Serb](/wiki/Serbs_in_Croatia "Serbs in Croatia") forces or fled the violence.[[268]](#cite_note-273) During the war's final days, about 150–200,000 Serbs fled before the arrival of Croatian forces during [Operation Storm](/wiki/Operation_Storm "Operation Storm").[[122]](#cite_note-bbc-storm-127)[[269]](#cite_note-274) After the war, the number of displaced persons fell to about 250,000. The Croatian government cared for displaced persons via the social security system and the Office of Displaced Persons and Refugees.[[270]](#cite_note-275) Most of the territories abandoned during the war were settled by Croat refugees from Bosnia and Herzegovina, mostly from north-western Bosnia, while some displaced people returned to their homes.[[271]](#cite_note-Index-Cro-Refugees-276)[[272]](#cite_note-PresidentRH-refugees-277)

According to the 2013 United Nations report, 17.6% of Croatia's population were immigrants.[[273]](#cite_note-278) According to the 2021 census, the majority of inhabitants are Croats (91.6%), followed by Serbs (3.2%), [Bosniaks](/wiki/Bosniaks_of_Croatia "Bosniaks of Croatia") (0.62%), [Roma](/wiki/Romani_people_in_Croatia "Romani people in Croatia") (0.46%), [Albanians](/wiki/Albanians_of_Croatia "Albanians of Croatia") (0.36%), [Italians](/wiki/Italians_of_Croatia "Italians of Croatia") (0.36%), [Hungarians](/wiki/Hungarians_of_Croatia "Hungarians of Croatia") (0.27%), [Czechs](/wiki/Czechs_of_Croatia "Czechs of Croatia") (0.20%), [Slovenes](/wiki/Slovenes_of_Croatia "Slovenes of Croatia") (0.20%), [Slovaks](/wiki/Slovaks_of_Croatia "Slovaks of Croatia") (0.10%), [Macedonians](/wiki/Macedonians_of_Croatia "Macedonians of Croatia") (0.09%), [Germans](/wiki/Germans_of_Croatia "Germans of Croatia") (0.09%), [Montenegrins](/wiki/Montenegrins_of_Croatia "Montenegrins of Croatia") (0.08%), and others (1.56%).[[4]](#cite_note-Census_2021-7) Approximately 4 million Croats [live abroad](/wiki/Croatian_diaspora "Croatian diaspora").[[274]](#cite_note-279)
