### Religion

Main article: [Religion in Croatia](/wiki/Religion_in_Croatia "Religion in Croatia")

Further information: [List of cathedrals in Croatia](/wiki/List_of_cathedrals_in_Croatia "List of cathedrals in Croatia") and [Catholic Church in Croatia](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_Croatia "Catholic Church in Croatia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/HR-Sibenik-Kathedrale-01.jpg/250px-HR-Sibenik-Kathedrale-01.jpg)](/wiki/File:HR-Sibenik-Kathedrale-01.jpg)

[Šibenik Cathedral](/wiki/%C5%A0ibenik_Cathedral "Šibenik Cathedral"), since 2000 on the [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") [World Heritage List](/wiki/World_Heritage_List "World Heritage List")

Croatia has no official religion. [Freedom of religion](/wiki/Freedom_of_religion "Freedom of religion") is a Constitutional right that protects all [religious communities](/wiki/Religious_denomination "Religious denomination") as [equal before the law and considers them separated from the state](/wiki/Separation_of_church_and_state "Separation of church and state").

According to the 2021 census, 87.39% of Croatians identify as Christian; of these, Catholics make up the largest group, accounting for 78.97% of the population, after which follows [Eastern Orthodoxy](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodoxy "Eastern Orthodoxy") (3.32%), [Protestantism](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") (0.26%), and other Christians (4.83%). The largest religion after Christianity is [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") (1.32%). 4.71% of the population describe itself as non-religious.[[276]](#cite_note-Census2011-religion-281) In the [Eurostat](/wiki/Eurostat "Eurostat") Eurobarometer Poll of 2010, 69% of the population responded that "they believe there is a God".[[277]](#cite_note-282) In a 2009 Gallup poll, 70% answered yes to the question "Is religion an important part of your daily life?"[[278]](#cite_note-283) Yet, only 24% of the population attends religious services regularly.[[279]](#cite_note-Pew_Survey_2017_–_Final_Topline-284)

### Languages

Main articles: [Croatian language](/wiki/Croatian_language "Croatian language"), [Languages of Croatia](/wiki/Languages_of_Croatia "Languages of Croatia"), [Minority languages of Croatia](/wiki/Minority_languages_of_Croatia "Minority languages of Croatia"), and [Bunjevac dialect](/wiki/Bunjevac_dialect "Bunjevac dialect")

See also: [Shtokavian dialect](/wiki/Shtokavian_dialect "Shtokavian dialect"), [Chakavian dialect](/wiki/Chakavian_dialect "Chakavian dialect"), and [Kajkavian dialect](/wiki/Kajkavian_dialect "Kajkavian dialect")

Croatian is the official language of the Republic of Croatia and has been an [official language of the European Union](/wiki/Official_languages_of_the_European_Union "Official languages of the European Union") since Croatia's accession in 2013.[[280]](#cite_note-Ustav-RH-285)[[281]](#cite_note-VL-Hrvatski-EU-286) Croatian replaced [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") as the official language of the Croatian government in the 19th century.[[282]](#cite_note-Iadertina-Hr-287) Following the [Vienna Literary Agreement](/wiki/Vienna_Literary_Agreement "Vienna Literary Agreement") in 1850, the language and its Latin alphabet underwent reforms to create an unified "Croatian or Serbian" or "[Serbo-Croatian](/wiki/Serbo-Croatian "Serbo-Croatian")" standard, which under various names became the official language of [Yugoslavia](/wiki/Yugoslavia "Yugoslavia").[[283]](#cite_note-288) In [SFR Yugoslavia](/wiki/Socialist_Federal_Republic_of_Yugoslavia "Socialist Federal Republic of Yugoslavia"), from 1972 to 1989, the language was constitutionally designated as the "Croatian literary language" and the "Croatian or Serbian language". It was the result of a resistance to and [secession](/wiki/Language_secessionism "Language secessionism") from "Serbo-Croatian" in the form of the [Declaration on the Status and Name of the Croatian Literary Language](/wiki/Declaration_on_the_Status_and_Name_of_the_Croatian_Literary_Language "Declaration on the Status and Name of the Croatian Literary Language") as part of the [Croatian Spring](/wiki/Croatian_Spring "Croatian Spring").[[284]](#cite_note-289) Since gaining independence in the early 1990s, the Republic of Croatia constitutionally designates the language as "Croatian language" and regulates it through [linguistic prescription](/wiki/Linguistic_prescription "Linguistic prescription"). The long-standing aspiration for development of its own expressions and thus enrichment of the language, as opposed to the adoption of foreign solutions in the form of [loanwords](/wiki/Loanwords "Loanwords"), has been described by linguists as [Croatian linguistic purism](/wiki/Croatian_linguistic_purism "Croatian linguistic purism").[[285]](#cite_note-290)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Croatian_dialects_in_Cro_and_BiH_1.PNG/250px-Croatian_dialects_in_Cro_and_BiH_1.PNG)](/wiki/File:Croatian_dialects_in_Cro_and_BiH_1.PNG)

Location map of dialects in Croatia and areas in Bosnia and Herzegovina with Croat majority. Chakavian in blue.

[Minority languages are in official use](/wiki/Minority_languages_of_Croatia "Minority languages of Croatia") in local government units where more than a third of the population consists of national minorities or where local enabling legislation applies. Those languages are [Czech](/wiki/Czech_language "Czech language"), [Hungarian](/wiki/Hungarian_language "Hungarian language"), [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language_in_Croatia "Italian language in Croatia"), [Serbian](/wiki/Serbian_language_in_Croatia "Serbian language in Croatia"), and [Slovak](/wiki/Slovak_language "Slovak language").[[286]](#cite_note-291)[[287]](#cite_note-Franceschini546-292) The following minority languages are also recognised: [Albanian](/wiki/Albanian_language "Albanian language"), [Bosnian](/wiki/Bosnian_language "Bosnian language"), [Bulgarian](/wiki/Bulgarian_language "Bulgarian language"), German, [Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew_language "Hebrew language"), [Macedonian](/wiki/Macedonian_language "Macedonian language"), [Montenegrin](/wiki/Montenegrin_language "Montenegrin language"), [Polish](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language"), [Romanian](/wiki/Romanian_language "Romanian language"), [Istro-Romanian](/wiki/Istro-Romanian_language "Istro-Romanian language"), [Romani](/wiki/Romani_language "Romani language"), Russian, [Rusyn](/wiki/Rusyn_language "Rusyn language"), [Slovene](/wiki/Slovene_language "Slovene language"), [Turkish](/wiki/Turkish_language "Turkish language"), and [Ukrainian](/wiki/Ukrainian_language "Ukrainian language").[[287]](#cite_note-Franceschini546-292)

According to the 2021 Census, 95.25% of citizens declared Croatian as their native language, 1.16% declared Serbian as their native language, while no other language reaches 0.26%.[[288]](#cite_note-Census-2011-languages-293) Croatian is a member of the [South Slavic languages](/wiki/South_Slavic_languages "South Slavic languages") and is written using the [Latin alphabet](/wiki/Latin_alphabet "Latin alphabet"). There are three major dialects spoken on the territory of Croatia, with standard Croatian based on the [Shtokavian](/wiki/Shtokavian "Shtokavian") dialect. The [Chakavian](/wiki/Chakavian "Chakavian") and [Kajkavian](/wiki/Kajkavian "Kajkavian") dialects are distinguished from Shtokavian by their [lexicon](/wiki/Lexicon "Lexicon"), [phonology](/wiki/Phonology "Phonology") and [syntax](/wiki/Syntax "Syntax").[[289]](#cite_note-Brozović-HRV-294)

A 2011 survey revealed that 78% of Croats claim knowledge of at least one foreign language.[[290]](#cite_note-Index-lang-295) According to a 2005 EC survey, 49% of Croats speak English as the second language, 34% speak German, 14% speak Italian, 10% speak French, 4% speak Russian and 2% speak Spanish. However several large municipalities support [minority languages](/wiki/Minority_languages_of_Croatia "Minority languages of Croatia"). A majority of Slovenes (59%) have some knowledge of Croatian.[[291]](#cite_note-EBS-243-296) The country is a part of various language-based international associations, most notably the [European Union Language Association.](/wiki/Languages_of_the_European_Union "Languages of the European Union")[[292]](#cite_note-297)

### Education

Main article: [Education in Croatia](/wiki/Education_in_Croatia "Education in Croatia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/National_and_University_Library_in_Zagreb.jpg/250px-National_and_University_Library_in_Zagreb.jpg)](/wiki/File:National_and_University_Library_in_Zagreb.jpg)

[National and University Library](/wiki/National_and_University_Library_in_Zagreb "National and University Library in Zagreb")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/University_of_Zagreb.jpg/250px-University_of_Zagreb.jpg)](/wiki/File:University_of_Zagreb.jpg)

[University of Zagreb](/wiki/University_of_Zagreb "University of Zagreb") is the largest Croatian university and the oldest university in the area covering Central Europe south of [Vienna](/wiki/Vienna "Vienna") and all of Southeastern Europe.

As of 2021, the literacy rate in Croatia was 99.45%.[[293]](#cite_note-298)[[294]](#cite_note-299) Primary education in Croatia starts at the age of six or seven and consists of eight grades. In 2007 a law was passed to increase free, noncompulsory education until 18 years of age. Compulsory education consists of eight grades of elementary school.

Secondary education is provided by [gymnasiums](/wiki/Gymnasium_(school) "Gymnasium (school)") and vocational schools. As of the 2022/2023 school year, there were 2,073 elementary schools and 738 upper secondary schools in Croatia.[[295]](#cite_note-300) Primary and secondary education are also available in languages of recognised minorities in Croatia, where classes are held in Czech, Hungarian, Italian, Serbian, German and Slovak languages.[[296]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTE2018_Statistical_Yearbook_of_the_Republic_of_Croatia488–489-301)

There are 133 elementary and secondary level [music](/wiki/Music_school "Music school") and [art schools](/wiki/Art_schools "Art schools"),[[297]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTE2018_Statistical_Yearbook_of_the_Republic_of_Croatia486-302) as well as 83 elementary and 44 secondary schools for disabled children and youth[[298]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTE2018_Statistical_Yearbook_of_the_Republic_of_Croatia484-485-303) and 11 elementary and 52 secondary schools for adults.[[299]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTE2018_Statistical_Yearbook_of_the_Republic_of_Croatia485-304) Nationwide [leaving exams](/wiki/Leaving_exams "Leaving exams") ([Croatian](/wiki/Croatian_language "Croatian language"): *državna matura*) were introduced for secondary education students in the school year 2009–2010. It comprises three compulsory subjects (Croatian language, mathematics, and a foreign language) and optional subjects and is a prerequisite for university education.[[300]](#cite_note-MZOS-Matura-305)
Croatia has eight public universities and two private universities.[[301]](#cite_note-iro-306) The [University of Zadar](/wiki/University_of_Zadar "University of Zadar"), the first university in Croatia, was founded in 1396 and remained active until 1807, when other institutions of higher education took over until the foundation of the renewed University of Zadar in 2002.[[302]](#cite_note-Zadar-Uni-307) The [University of Zagreb](/wiki/University_of_Zagreb "University of Zagreb"), founded in 1669, is the oldest continuously operating university in Southeast Europe.[[303]](#cite_note-Zagreb-Uni-308) There are also 15 [polytechnics](/wiki/Institute_of_technology "Institute of technology"), of which two are private, and 30 higher education institutions, of which 27 are private.[[301]](#cite_note-iro-306) In total, there are 131 institutions of higher education in Croatia, attended by more than 160 thousand students.[[304]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTE2018_Statistical_Yearbook_of_the_Republic_of_Croatia490-309)

In 2022, Croatia's [research and development](/wiki/Research_and_development "Research and development") (R&D) expenditure was approximately 1.43% of GDP. Among the scientific institutes operating in Croatia, the largest is the [Ruđer Bošković Institute](/wiki/Ru%C4%91er_Bo%C5%A1kovi%C4%87_Institute "Ruđer Bošković Institute") in Zagreb.[[305]](#cite_note-310) The [Croatian Academy of Sciences and Arts](/wiki/Croatian_Academy_of_Sciences_and_Arts "Croatian Academy of Sciences and Arts") in Zagreb is a [learned society](/wiki/Learned_society "Learned society") promoting language, culture, arts and science from its inception in 1866.[[306]](#cite_note-HAZU-founding-311) Croatia was ranked 40th in the [Global Innovation Index](/wiki/Global_Innovation_Index "Global Innovation Index") in 2025.[[307]](#cite_note-312)[[308]](#cite_note-313)

The [European Investment Bank](/wiki/European_Investment_Bank "European Investment Bank") provided digital infrastructure and equipment to around 150 primary and secondary schools in Croatia. Twenty of these schools got specialised assistance in the form of gear, software, and services to help them integrate the teaching and administrative operations.[[309]](#cite_note-314)[[310]](#cite_note-315) In 2024, the EIB extended a €207 million loan to the [City of Zagreb](/wiki/Zagreb "Zagreb") for infrastructure upgrades, including investments in renewable energy, energy efficiency projects, social and affordable housing, schools, and kindergartens.[[311]](#cite_note-European_Investment_Bank-316)[[312]](#cite_note-European_Investment_Bank_2-317) Additionally, the EIB signed a €49 million loan with the [City of Split](/wiki/Split,_Croatia "Split, Croatia") to co-finance its 2023–2027 multi-sector investment programme, which includes refurbishing public buildings, increasing energy efficiency, and improving technical equipment for public information and communication.[[311]](#cite_note-European_Investment_Bank-316)[[312]](#cite_note-European_Investment_Bank_2-317)

### Healthcare

Main article: [Healthcare in Croatia](/wiki/Healthcare_in_Croatia "Healthcare in Croatia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/KBC_Rebro_aerial.jpg/250px-KBC_Rebro_aerial.jpg)](/wiki/File:KBC_Rebro_aerial.jpg)

[University Hospital Centre Zagreb](/wiki/University_Hospital_Centre_Zagreb "University Hospital Centre Zagreb") is the largest hospital in Croatia and the teaching hospital of the [University of Zagreb](/wiki/University_of_Zagreb "University of Zagreb").

Croatia has a [universal health care](/wiki/Universal_health_care "Universal health care") system, whose roots can be traced back to the Hungarian-Croatian Parliament Act of 1891, providing a form of mandatory insurance of all factory workers and craftsmen.[[313]](#cite_note-RSP-1891-318) The population is covered by a basic health insurance plan provided by statute and optional insurance. In 2017, annual healthcare related expenditures reached 22.2 billion kuna (around €3.0 billion).[[314]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTE2018_Statistical_Yearbook_of_the_Republic_of_Croatia546-319) Healthcare expenditures comprise only 0.6% of private health insurance and public spending.[[315]](#cite_note-Vjesnik-Zdravstvo-320) In 2022, Croatia spent around 7.2% of its GDP on healthcare.[[316]](#cite_note-321)
In 2025, Croatia ranked 53rd in the world in life expectancy with 75.8 years for men and 81.9 years for women, and it had a low [infant mortality rate](/wiki/Infant_mortality_rate "Infant mortality rate") of 3.3 per 1,000 [live births](/wiki/Live_birth_(human) "Live birth (human)").[[317]](#cite_note-322)

There are hundreds of healthcare institutions in Croatia, including 75 hospitals, and 13 clinics with 23,049 beds. The hospitals and clinics care for more than 700 thousand patients per year and employ 6,642 [medical doctors](/wiki/Medical_doctors "Medical doctors"), including 4,773 specialists.[[318]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTE2018_Statistical_Yearbook_of_the_Republic_of_Croatia525-323) There is a total of 69,841 health workers.[[319]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTE2018_Statistical_Yearbook_of_the_Republic_of_Croatia524-324) There are 119 emergency units in health centres, responding to more than a million calls.[[320]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTE2018_Statistical_Yearbook_of_the_Republic_of_Croatia532-325) The principal cause of death in 2022 was [cardiovascular disease](/wiki/Cardiovascular_disease "Cardiovascular disease") at 34.3% for men and 43.8% for women, followed by [hypertensive](/wiki/Hypertension "Hypertension") disease.[[321]](#cite_note-326) In 2022 it was estimated that 37% of Croatians are smokers.[[322]](#cite_note-327) According to 2022 data, 35.65% of the Croatian adult population is obese.[[323]](#cite_note-328)

## Culture

Main article: [Culture of Croatia](/wiki/Culture_of_Croatia "Culture of Croatia")

Further information: [World Heritage Sites in Croatia](/wiki/World_Heritage_Sites_in_Croatia "World Heritage Sites in Croatia"), [Register of Cultural Goods of Croatia](/wiki/Register_of_Cultural_Goods_of_Croatia "Register of Cultural Goods of Croatia"), and [List of museums in Croatia](/wiki/List_of_museums_in_Croatia "List of museums in Croatia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/Trogir_promen%C3%A1da_B%C3%A1na_Berislavi%C4%87e.jpg/250px-Trogir_promen%C3%A1da_B%C3%A1na_Berislavi%C4%87e.jpg)](/wiki/File:Trogir_promen%C3%A1da_B%C3%A1na_Berislavi%C4%87e.jpg)

The historic centre of [Trogir](/wiki/Trogir "Trogir") has been included in the [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") list of [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site") since 1997.[[324]](#cite_note-329)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Pola%2C_anfiteatro%2C_01.jpg/250px-Pola%2C_anfiteatro%2C_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pola,_anfiteatro,_01.jpg)

[Pula Arena](/wiki/Pula_Arena "Pula Arena"), Roman amphitheatre located in [Pula](/wiki/Pula "Pula"), constructed between 27 BC and AD 68.

Because of its geographical position, Croatia represents a blend of four different cultural spheres. It has been a crossroads of influences from western culture and the east since the schism between the [Western Roman Empire](/wiki/Western_Roman_Empire "Western Roman Empire") and the [Byzantine Empire](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire"), and also from Central Europe and [Mediterranean culture](/wiki/Mediterranean_culture "Mediterranean culture").[[325]](#cite_note-HTZ-History-Culture-330) The [Illyrian movement](/wiki/Illyrian_movement "Illyrian movement") was the most significant period of national cultural history, as the 19th century proved crucial to the emancipation of Croatians and saw unprecedented developments in all fields of art and culture, giving rise to many [historical figures](/wiki/List_of_people_from_Croatia "List of people from Croatia").[[60]](#cite_note-CRIS-Stančić-65)

The [Ministry of Culture](/wiki/Ministry_of_Culture_(Croatia) "Ministry of Culture (Croatia)") is tasked with preserving the nation's [cultural and natural heritage](/wiki/Natural_and_Cultural_Heritage_of_Croatia "Natural and Cultural Heritage of Croatia") and overseeing its development. Further activities supporting the development of culture are undertaken at the local government level.[[326]](#cite_note-MKult-Djelokrug-331) The [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO")'s [World Heritage List](/wiki/World_Heritage_Sites_by_country "World Heritage Sites by country") includes [ten sites in Croatia](/wiki/Ten_sites_in_Croatia "Ten sites in Croatia") and a list of Intangible Cultural Heritage of Croatia.[[327]](#cite_note-332) The country is also rich with [intangible culture](/wiki/Intangible_culture "Intangible culture") and holds 15 of [UNESCO's World's intangible culture masterpieces](/wiki/UNESCO%27s_World%27s_intangible_culture_masterpieces "UNESCO's World's intangible culture masterpieces"), ranking fourth in the world.[[328]](#cite_note-333) A global cultural contribution from Croatia is the necktie, derived from the [cravat](/wiki/Cravat_(early) "Cravat (early)") originally worn by the 17th-century Croatian mercenaries in France.[[329]](#cite_note-NYT-tie-334)[[330]](#cite_note-Huzjan-335)

In 2019, Croatia had 95 professional theatres, 30 professional children's theatres, and 51 amateur theatres visited by more than 2.27 million viewers per year. Professional theatres employ 1,195 artists. There are 42 professional orchestras, ensembles, and choirs, attracting an annual attendance of 297 thousand. There are 75 cinemas with 166 screens and attendance of 5.026 million.[[331]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTE2018_Statistical_Yearbook_of_the_Republic_of_Croatia512–513-336)

Croatia has 222 museums, visited by more than 2.71 million people in 2016. Furthermore, there are 1,768 libraries, containing 26.8 million volumes, and 19 state archives.[[332]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTE2018_Statistical_Yearbook_of_the_Republic_of_Croatia516–521-337) The book publishing market is dominated by several major publishers and the industry's centrepiece event—[Interliber](/wiki/Interliber "Interliber") exhibition held annually at [Zagreb Fair](/wiki/Zagreb_Fair "Zagreb Fair").[[333]](#cite_note-JL-Interliber-338)

### Arts, literature, and music

Main articles: [Croatian art](/wiki/Croatian_art "Croatian art"), [Architecture of Croatia](/wiki/Architecture_of_Croatia "Architecture of Croatia"), [Croatian literature](/wiki/Croatian_literature "Croatian literature"), and [Croatian music](/wiki/Croatian_music "Croatian music")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/Peristyle%2C_Split_2.jpg/250px-Peristyle%2C_Split_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Peristyle,_Split_2.jpg)

Historical nucleus of [Split](/wiki/Split,_Croatia "Split, Croatia") with the 4th-century [Diocletian's Palace](/wiki/Diocletian%27s_Palace "Diocletian's Palace") was inscribed on the [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") list of [World Heritage Sites](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site") in 1979.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/EuphrasiusBasilika.jpg/120px-EuphrasiusBasilika.jpg)](/wiki/File:EuphrasiusBasilika.jpg)

[Euphrasian Basilica](/wiki/Euphrasian_Basilica "Euphrasian Basilica") in [Poreč](/wiki/Pore%C4%8D "Poreč"), example of early [Byzantine architecture](/wiki/Byzantine_architecture "Byzantine architecture"), on the [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") [World Heritage List](/wiki/World_Heritage_List "World Heritage List") since 1997

Architecture in Croatia reflects influences of bordering nations. Austrian and Hungarian influence is visible in public spaces and buildings in the north and the central regions, architecture found along coasts of Dalmatia and Istria exhibits Venetian influence.[[334]](#cite_note-339) Squares named after culture heroes, parks, and pedestrian-only zones, are features of Croatian towns and cities, especially where large scale [Baroque](/wiki/Baroque_architecture "Baroque architecture") urban planning took place, for instance in Osijek ([Tvrđa](/wiki/Tvr%C4%91a "Tvrđa")), Varaždin, and Karlovac.[[335]](#cite_note-Telegraph-Baroque-340)[[336]](#cite_note-JL-Karlovac-Baroque-341) The subsequent influence of the [Art Nouveau](/wiki/Art_Nouveau "Art Nouveau") was reflected in contemporary architecture.[[337]](#cite_note-IPU-Art-Nouveau-342) The architecture is the Mediterranean with a Venetian and Renaissance influence in major coastal urban areas exemplified in works of [Giorgio da Sebenico](/wiki/Giorgio_da_Sebenico "Giorgio da Sebenico") and [Nicolas of Florence](/w/index.php?title=Nicolas_of_Florence&action=edit&redlink=1 "Nicolas of Florence (page does not exist)") such as the [Cathedral of St. James](/wiki/%C5%A0ibenik_Cathedral "Šibenik Cathedral") in Šibenik. The oldest preserved examples of Croatian architecture are the 9th-century churches, with the largest and the most representative among them being [Church of St. Donatus](/wiki/Church_of_St._Donatus "Church of St. Donatus") in [Zadar](/wiki/Zadar "Zadar").[[338]](#cite_note-MVPEI-Art-343)[[339]](#cite_note-TZZadar-Donat-344)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/Marco_Marulo_1903_Ljetopis_dru%C5%A1tva_hrvatskih_knji%C5%BEevnika_za_1900-1903.jpg/250px-Marco_Marulo_1903_Ljetopis_dru%C5%A1tva_hrvatskih_knji%C5%BEevnika_za_1900-1903.jpg)](/wiki/File:Marco_Marulo_1903_Ljetopis_dru%C5%A1tva_hrvatskih_knji%C5%BEevnika_za_1900-1903.jpg)

[Marko Marulić](/wiki/Marko_Maruli%C4%87 "Marko Marulić") (18 August 1450 – 5 January 1524), Croatian poet, lawyer, judge, and Renaissance humanist who coined the term "psychology". He is the [national poet](/wiki/National_poet "National poet") of Croatia.

Besides the architecture encompassing the oldest artworks, there is a history of artists in Croatia reaching the Middle Ages. In that period the stone portal of the [Trogir Cathedral](/wiki/Trogir_Cathedral "Trogir Cathedral") was made by [Radovan](/wiki/Radovan_(master) "Radovan (master)"), representing the most important monument of [Romanesque](/wiki/Romanesque_art "Romanesque art") sculpture from [Medieval Croatia](/wiki/Croatia_in_the_union_with_Hungary "Croatia in the union with Hungary"). The [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance_in_Croatia "Renaissance in Croatia") had the greatest impact on the Adriatic Sea coast since the remainder was embroiled in the Hundred Years' Croatian–Ottoman War. With the waning of the Ottoman Empire, art flourished during the [Baroque](/wiki/Baroque "Baroque") and [Rococo](/wiki/Rococo "Rococo"). The 19th and 20th centuries brought affirmation of numerous Croatian artisans, helped by several patrons of the arts such as bishop [Josip Juraj Strossmayer](/wiki/Josip_Juraj_Strossmayer "Josip Juraj Strossmayer").[[340]](#cite_note-Essehist-Strossmayer-345) Croatian artists of the period achieving renown were [Vlaho Bukovac](/wiki/Vlaho_Bukovac "Vlaho Bukovac"), [Ivan Meštrović](/wiki/Ivan_Me%C5%A1trovi%C4%87 "Ivan Meštrović"), and [Ivan Generalić](/wiki/Ivan_Generali%C4%87 "Ivan Generalić").[[338]](#cite_note-MVPEI-Art-343)[[341]](#cite_note-Hintz_2004-346)

The Baška tablet, a stone inscribed with the [glagolitic alphabet](/wiki/Glagolitic_alphabet "Glagolitic alphabet") found on the Krk island and dated to c. 1100, is considered to be the oldest surviving prose in Croatian.[[342]](#cite_note-KRK-Baška-347) The beginning of more vigorous development of Croatian literature is marked by the Renaissance and [Marko Marulić](/wiki/Marko_Maruli%C4%87 "Marko Marulić"). Besides Marulić, Renaissance playwright [Marin Držić](/wiki/Marin_Dr%C5%BEi%C4%87 "Marin Držić"), Baroque poet [Ivan Gundulić](/wiki/Ivan_Gunduli%C4%87 "Ivan Gundulić"), [Croatian national revival](/wiki/Croatian_national_revival "Croatian national revival") poet [Ivan Mažuranić](/wiki/Ivan_Ma%C5%BEurani%C4%87 "Ivan Mažuranić"), novelist, playwright, and poet [August Šenoa](/wiki/August_%C5%A0enoa "August Šenoa"), children's writer [Ivana Brlić-Mažuranić](/wiki/Ivana_Brli%C4%87-Ma%C5%BEurani%C4%87 "Ivana Brlić-Mažuranić"), writer and journalist [Marija Jurić Zagorka](/wiki/Marija_Juri%C4%87_Zagorka "Marija Jurić Zagorka"), poet and writer [Antun Gustav Matoš](/wiki/Antun_Gustav_Mato%C5%A1 "Antun Gustav Matoš"), poet [Antun Branko Šimić](/wiki/Antun_Branko_%C5%A0imi%C4%87 "Antun Branko Šimić"), [expressionist](/wiki/Expressionist "Expressionist") and [realist](/wiki/Realism_(arts) "Realism (arts)") writer [Miroslav Krleža](/wiki/Miroslav_Krle%C5%BEa "Miroslav Krleža"), poet [Tin Ujević](/wiki/Tin_Ujevi%C4%87 "Tin Ujević") and novelist, and short story writer [Ivo Andrić](/wiki/Ivo_Andri%C4%87 "Ivo Andrić") are often cited as the greatest figures in Croatian literature.[[343]](#cite_note-LZMK-348)[[344]](#cite_note-NYT-Readerguide-349)

Croatian music varies from classical operas to modern-day rock. [Vatroslav Lisinski](/wiki/Vatroslav_Lisinski "Vatroslav Lisinski") created the country's first opera, *Love and Malice*, in 1846. [Ivan Zajc](/wiki/Ivan_Zajc "Ivan Zajc") composed more than a thousand pieces of music, including masses and oratorios. Pianist [Ivo Pogorelić](/wiki/Ivo_Pogoreli%C4%87 "Ivo Pogorelić") has performed across the world.[[341]](#cite_note-Hintz_2004-346)

### Media

Main articles: [Media of Croatia](/wiki/Media_of_Croatia "Media of Croatia"), [Cinema of Croatia](/wiki/Cinema_of_Croatia "Cinema of Croatia"), [Television in Croatia](/wiki/Television_in_Croatia "Television in Croatia"), and [List of radio stations in Croatia](/wiki/List_of_radio_stations_in_Croatia "List of radio stations in Croatia")

In Croatia, the Constitution guarantees the freedom of the press and the freedom of speech.[[345]](#cite_note-worldpress-350) Croatia ranked 64th in the 2019 [Press Freedom Index](/wiki/Press_Freedom_Index "Press Freedom Index") report compiled by [Reporters Without Borders](/wiki/Reporters_Without_Borders "Reporters Without Borders") which noted that journalists who investigate corruption, organised crime or war crimes face challenges and that the Government was trying to influence the public broadcaster [HRT](/wiki/Croatian_Radiotelevision "Croatian Radiotelevision")'s editorial policies.[[346]](#cite_note-index-351) In its 2019 Freedom in the World report, the [Freedom House](/wiki/Freedom_House "Freedom House") classified freedoms of press and speech in Croatia as generally free from political interference and manipulation, noting that journalists still face threats and occasional attacks.[[347]](#cite_note-352) The state-owned news agency [HINA](/wiki/HINA "HINA") runs a [wire service](/wiki/Wire_service "Wire service") in Croatian and English on politics, economics, society, and culture.[[348]](#cite_note-HINA-about-353)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/Zgrada_HRT_Zagreb.jpg/250px-Zgrada_HRT_Zagreb.jpg)](/wiki/File:Zgrada_HRT_Zagreb.jpg)

[Radio Zagreb](/wiki/Croatian_Radio "Croatian Radio"), now a part of [Croatian](/wiki/Croatian_language "Croatian language") national [public broadcasting](/wiki/Public_broadcasting "Public broadcasting") company, [Croatian Radiotelevision](/wiki/Croatian_Radiotelevision "Croatian Radiotelevision"), was the first public radio station in Southeast Europe.[[349]](#cite_note-354)

As of January 2021[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Croatia&action=edit), there are thirteen nationwide free-to-air [DVB-T](/wiki/DVB-T "DVB-T") television channels, with [Croatian Radiotelevision](/wiki/Croatian_Radiotelevision "Croatian Radiotelevision") (HRT) operating four, [RTL Televizija](/wiki/RTL_(Croatian_TV_channel) "RTL (Croatian TV channel)") three, and [Nova TV](/wiki/Nova_TV_(Croatia) "Nova TV (Croatia)") operating two channels, and the [Croatian Olympic Committee](/wiki/Croatian_Olympic_Committee "Croatian Olympic Committee"), Kapital Net d.o.o., and Author d.o.o. companies operate the remaining three.[[350]](#cite_note-355) Also, there are 21 regional or local DVB-T television channels.[[351]](#cite_note-OIV-DVBT-356) The HRT is also broadcasting a satellite TV channel.[[352]](#cite_note-HRT-Sat-357) In 2020, there were 147 radio stations and 27 TV stations in Croatia.[[353]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTE2018_Statistical_Yearbook_of_the_Republic_of_Croatia510-358)[[354]](#cite_note-359) Cable television and [IPTV](/wiki/IPTV "IPTV") networks are gaining ground. Cable television already serves 450 thousand people, around 10% of the total population of the country.[[355]](#cite_note-Lider-IPTV-360)[[356]](#cite_note-JL-cableTV-361)

In 2010, 267 newspapers and 2,676 magazines were published in Croatia.[[353]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTE2018_Statistical_Yearbook_of_the_Republic_of_Croatia510-358) The print media market is dominated by the Croatian-owned [Hanza Media](/wiki/Hanza_Media "Hanza Media") and Austrian-owned [Styria Media Group](/wiki/Styria_Media_Group "Styria Media Group") who publish their flagship dailies *[Jutarnji list](/wiki/Jutarnji_list "Jutarnji list")*, *[Večernji list](/wiki/Ve%C4%8Dernji_list "Večernji list")* and *[24sata](/wiki/24sata_(Croatia) "24sata (Croatia)")*. Other influential newspapers are *[Novi list](/wiki/Novi_list "Novi list")* and *[Slobodna Dalmacija](/wiki/Slobodna_Dalmacija "Slobodna Dalmacija")*.[[357]](#cite_note-EPH-Print-362)[[358]](#cite_note-Styria-dailies-363) In 2020, *24sata* was the most widely circulated daily newspaper, followed by *Večernji list* and *Jutarnji list*.[[359]](#cite_note-364)[[360]](#cite_note-365)

[Croatia](/wiki/Croatia_in_the_Eurovision_Song_Contest "Croatia in the Eurovision Song Contest") competed in the [Eurovision Song Contest](/wiki/Eurovision_Song_Contest "Eurovision Song Contest") as part of [Yugoslavia](/wiki/Yugoslavia_in_the_Eurovision_Song_Contest "Yugoslavia in the Eurovision Song Contest") since 1961. The first and only victory Yugoslavia achieved in the competition was accomplished by the Croatian pop band [Riva](/wiki/Riva_(band) "Riva (band)") in 1989. Since its debut at the [1993 contest](/wiki/Eurovision_Song_Contest_1993 "Eurovision Song Contest 1993"), Croatia won two fourth places at the [1996](/wiki/Eurovision_Song_Contest_1996 "Eurovision Song Contest 1996") and [1999](/wiki/Eurovision_Song_Contest_1999 "Eurovision Song Contest 1999") contests, and one second place at the [2024 contest](/wiki/Eurovision_Song_Contest_2024 "Eurovision Song Contest 2024"), marking the country's best result to date as an independent nation.[[361]](#cite_note-366)

### Film industry

Main articles: [Cinema of Croatia](/wiki/Cinema_of_Croatia "Cinema of Croatia") and [Television in Croatia](/wiki/Television_in_Croatia "Television in Croatia")

Croatia's film industry is small and heavily subsidised by the government, mainly through grants approved by the Ministry of Culture with films often being co-produced by HRT.[[362]](#cite_note-JL-Film-367)[[363]](#cite_note-HRT-coproducing-368) Croatian cinema produces between five and ten feature films per year.[[364]](#cite_note-369) [Pula Film Festival](/wiki/Pula_Film_Festival "Pula Film Festival"), the national film awards event held annually in [Pula](/wiki/Pula "Pula"), is the most prestigious film event featuring national and international productions.[[365]](#cite_note-Vjesnik-PulaFilm-370) [Animafest Zagreb](/wiki/Animafest_Zagreb "Animafest Zagreb"), founded in 1972, is the prestigious annual film festival dedicated to the animated film. The first greatest accomplishment by Croatian filmmakers was achieved by [Dušan Vukotić](/wiki/Du%C5%A1an_Vukoti%C4%87 "Dušan Vukotić") when he won the 1961 [Academy Award for Best Animated Short Film](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Animated_Short_Film "Academy Award for Best Animated Short Film") for *[Ersatz](/wiki/Surogat "Surogat")* ([Croatian](/wiki/Croatian_language "Croatian language"): *Surogat*).[[366]](#cite_note-Vjesnik-surogat-371) Croatian film producer [Branko Lustig](/wiki/Branko_Lustig "Branko Lustig") won the [Academy Awards for Best Picture](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Picture "Academy Award for Best Picture") for *[Schindler's List](/wiki/Schindler%27s_List "Schindler's List")* and *[Gladiator](/wiki/Gladiator_(2000_film) "Gladiator (2000 film)")*.[[367]](#cite_note-Film_Producer_Branko_Lustig_Becomes_Honorary_Citizen_of_Zagreb-372) In addition to that, Croatian filmmaker [Nebojša Slijepčević](/wiki/Neboj%C5%A1a_Slijep%C4%8Devi%C4%87 "Nebojša Slijepčević") got nominated for [97th Academy Awards](/wiki/97th_Academy_Awards "97th Academy Awards") in category for [Best Live Action Short Film](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Live_Action_Short_Film "Academy Award for Best Live Action Short Film") for his 2024 movie *[The Man Who Could Not Remain Silent](/wiki/The_Man_Who_Could_Not_Remain_Silent "The Man Who Could Not Remain Silent")* ([Croatian](/wiki/Croatian_language "Croatian language"): *Čovjek koji nije mogao šutjeti*), making it first Croatian nomination in that category, and first since its [independence](/wiki/Croatian_independence "Croatian independence").[[368]](#cite_note-373)

Before and since its [independence](/wiki/Independence_of_Croatia "Independence of Croatia"), Croatia has become a popular filming destination amongs international filming productions, and a lot of [blockbuster](/wiki/Blockbuster_(entertainment) "Blockbuster (entertainment)") films and TV series have been filmed in Croatia including: *[Game of Thrones](/wiki/Game_of_Thrones "Game of Thrones")*, *[Star Wars: The Last Jedi](/wiki/Star_Wars:_The_Last_Jedi "Star Wars: The Last Jedi")*, *[Robin Hood](/wiki/Robin_Hood_(2018_film) "Robin Hood (2018 film)")* in Dubrovnik, *[Speak No Evil](/wiki/Speak_No_Evil_(2024_film) "Speak No Evil (2024 film)")*, *[Season of the Witch](/wiki/Season_of_the_Witch_(2011_film) "Season of the Witch (2011 film)")* and *[Titius](/wiki/Titus_(film) "Titus (film)")* in Istria, *[Infinity Pool](/wiki/Infinity_Pool_(film) "Infinity Pool (film)")* in Šibenik, *[Canary Black](/wiki/Canary_Black "Canary Black")*, *[Hitman's Wife's Bodyguard](/wiki/Hitman%27s_Wife%27s_Bodyguard "Hitman's Wife's Bodyguard")*, *[Sophie's Choice](/wiki/Sophie%27s_Choice_(film) "Sophie's Choice (film)")*, *[Armour of God](/wiki/Armour_of_God_(film) "Armour of God (film)")* and *[Fiddler on the roof](/wiki/Fiddler_on_the_Roof_(film) "Fiddler on the Roof (film)")* in Zagreb, *[Mamma Mia! Here We Go Again](/wiki/Mamma_Mia!_Here_We_Go_Again "Mamma Mia! Here We Go Again")* on island of Vis, *[Succession](/wiki/Succession_(TV_series) "Succession (TV series)")* on the island of Korčula, *[Hercules](/wiki/Hercules_(2014_film) "Hercules (2014 film)")*, *[The Weekend Away](/wiki/The_Weekend_Away "The Weekend Away")*, *[Bliss](/wiki/Bliss_(2021_film) "Bliss (2021 film)")* in Split, *[The Peacemaker](/wiki/The_Peacemaker_(1997_film) "The Peacemaker (1997 film)")*, *[Lee](/wiki/Lee_(2023_film) "Lee (2023 film)")* and many others.[[369]](#cite_note-374)[[370]](#cite_note-375) Croatia became international filming location due to its biodiversity, landscape that can accommodate every visual requirements and cheaper filming costs.[[371]](#cite_note-376)[[372]](#cite_note-377) In last 11 years there has been 122 projects for international films in Croatia, and €263 million spent as part of the Filming in Croatia which increased in last years due to high demand for its location.[[373]](#cite_note-378)

### Cuisine

Main articles: [Croatian cuisine](/wiki/Croatian_cuisine "Croatian cuisine") and [Croatian wine](/wiki/Croatian_wine "Croatian wine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/Kuhane_strukle_0210_1.jpg/250px-Kuhane_strukle_0210_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kuhane_strukle_0210_1.jpg)

[Zagorski štrukli](/wiki/Zagorski_%C5%A1trukli "Zagorski štrukli")

Croatian traditional cuisine varies from one region to another. Dalmatia and Istria have culinary influences of [Italian](/wiki/Italian_cuisine "Italian cuisine") and other [Mediterranean cuisines](/wiki/Mediterranean_cuisine "Mediterranean cuisine") which prominently feature various seafood, cooked vegetables and pasta, and condiments such as olive oil and garlic. [Austrian](/wiki/Austrian_cuisine "Austrian cuisine"), [Hungarian](/wiki/Hungarian_cuisine "Hungarian cuisine"), [Turkish](/wiki/Turkish_cuisine "Turkish cuisine"), and [Balkan](/wiki/Balkan_cuisine "Balkan cuisine") culinary styles influenced continental cuisine. In that area, meats, freshwater fish, and vegetable dishes are predominant.[[374]](#cite_note-HTZ-cuisine-379)

There are two distinct wine-producing regions in Croatia. The continental in the northeast of the country, especially Slavonia, produces premium wines, particularly whites. Along the north coast, Istrian and Krk wines are similar to those in neighbouring Italy, while further south in Dalmatia, Mediterranean-style red wines are the norm.[[374]](#cite_note-HTZ-cuisine-379) Annual production of wine exceeds 72 million litres as of 2017[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Croatia&action=edit).[[375]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTE2018_Statistical_Yearbook_of_the_Republic_of_Croatia265-380) Croatia was almost exclusively a wine-consuming country up until the late 18th century when a more massive beer production and consumption started.[[376]](#cite_note-skenderovic-381)

There are 11 restaurants in Croatia with a [Michelin star](/wiki/Michelin_star "Michelin star") and 89 restaurants bearing some of the Michelin's marks.[[377]](#cite_note-382)

### Sports

Main articles: [Sport in Croatia](/wiki/Sport_in_Croatia "Sport in Croatia") and [Croatia at the Olympics](/wiki/Croatia_at_the_Olympics "Croatia at the Olympics")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/Croatia_WC2018_final.jpg/330px-Croatia_WC2018_final.jpg)](/wiki/File:Croatia_WC2018_final.jpg)

[Croatia national football team](/wiki/Croatia_national_football_team "Croatia national football team") in [World Cup 2018](/wiki/World_Cup_2018 "World Cup 2018") in Russia

Croatia has a long tradition of sports dating back to Roman times, followed by popular medieval [knights' tournaments](/wiki/Tournament_(medieval) "Tournament (medieval)").[[378]](#cite_note-b918-383) Modern organized sports began in the late 19th century with the founding of sports associations like Hrvatski Sokol in 1874.[[378]](#cite_note-b918-383) By the early 20th century, many sports organizations emerged, including the [Croatian Sports Association](/wiki/Croatian_Sports_Association "Croatian Sports Association") established in 1909.[[379]](#cite_note-Ekon-384) Croatian sports' development has been closely related to the Olympic movement, with [Franjo Bučar](/wiki/Franjo_Bu%C4%8Dar "Franjo Bučar") playing a key role in promoting sports for [Croatian independence](/wiki/Independence_of_Croatia "Independence of Croatia").[[379]](#cite_note-Ekon-384) Sport is part of the school curriculum, and many children engage in extracurricular activities.[[380]](#cite_note-x676-385) To support sports development, Croatia enacted the National Sport Programme in 2019.[[381]](#cite_note-j068-386) Funding for sports comes from the state budget, sponsorship, and membership fees.[[382]](#cite_note-v584-387) The [Croatian Olympic Committee](/wiki/Croatian_Olympic_Committee "Croatian Olympic Committee"), established in 1991, oversees over 80 national sports associations.[[383]](#cite_note-f065-388)

The most popular sports in Croatia are football, basketball, handball, and water polo.[[378]](#cite_note-b918-383) With about 12,500 sports associations, [football is the most popular sport](/wiki/Football_in_Croatia "Football in Croatia"), hosting nearly 1,500 clubs and 110,000 players, achieving significant milestones, including bronze at the [1998 World Cup](/wiki/1998_World_Cup "1998 World Cup"), silver at the [2018 World Cup](/wiki/2018_World_Cup "2018 World Cup") and another bronze at [2022 World Cup](/wiki/2022_World_Cup "2022 World Cup").[[378]](#cite_note-b918-383) [Croatians have participated in the Olympics](/wiki/Croatia_at_the_Olympics "Croatia at the Olympics") since 1992 and have won [59 Olympic medals, including 20 gold](/wiki/Croatia_at_the_Olympics#Medals "Croatia at the Olympics").[[384]](#cite_note-y175-389) The [national tennis team](/wiki/Croatia_Davis_Cup_team "Croatia Davis Cup team") has won two [Davis Cup](/wiki/Davis_Cup "Davis Cup") titles and won a gold medal at the [2020 Summer Olympics](/wiki/Tennis_at_the_2020_Summer_Olympics "Tennis at the 2020 Summer Olympics") in men's doubles.[[385]](#cite_note-c001-390) The [national handball](/wiki/Croatia_men%27s_national_handball_team "Croatia men's national handball team") and [water polo teams](/wiki/Croatia_men%27s_national_water_polo_team "Croatia men's national water polo team") are also been successful, each achieving multiple championships and medals.[[386]](#cite_note-x798-391) [Rowing](/wiki/Croatian_Rowing_Federation "Croatian Rowing Federation") has seen success as well, winning multiple Olympic and World Championship medals.[[387]](#cite_note-d368-392) In gymnastics, athletes have also made their mark, winning medals in European and World Championships.[[388]](#cite_note-h046-393)

Croatia hosted several major sports competitions, including the [2009 World Men's Handball Championship](/wiki/2009_World_Men%27s_Handball_Championship "2009 World Men's Handball Championship"), the [2007 World Table Tennis Championships](/wiki/2007_World_Table_Tennis_Championships "2007 World Table Tennis Championships"), the [2000 World Rowing Championships](/wiki/2000_World_Rowing_Championships "2000 World Rowing Championships"), the [1987 Summer Universiade](/wiki/1987_Summer_Universiade "1987 Summer Universiade"), the [1979 Mediterranean Games](/wiki/1979_Mediterranean_Games "1979 Mediterranean Games"), and several [European Championships](/wiki/European_Championship "European Championship"), including the [2000](/wiki/2000_European_Men%27s_Handball_Championship "2000 European Men's Handball Championship"), [2018](/wiki/2018_European_Men%27s_Handball_Championship "2018 European Men's Handball Championship") and [2025 World Men's Handball Championship](/wiki/2025_World_Men%27s_Handball_Championship "2025 World Men's Handball Championship"), [2024 Men's European Water Polo Championship](/wiki/2024_Men%27s_European_Water_Polo_Championship "2024 Men's European Water Polo Championship").

"The Presidency of the Croatian Academy of Sciences and Arts, in a session held on 12 September 2014, made the following statement explaining that the Bunjevci Croats form an integral part of the Croatian national corpus. The Presidency of the Croatian Academy of Sciences and Arts, one of the fundamental institutions of the Croatian nation and of all the citizens of the Republic of Croatia, among the roles of which belongs the preservation of national identity, made the following statement in a session held on 12 September 2014: The Bunjevci, a Croatian ethnic group, are made up of three branches: the Dalmatian-Herzegovinian branch; the Primorje-Lika branch; and the Danube Region branch. Not encroaching on the right of any individual to express their national affiliation based on their origin, history, traditional culture, customs and language – the western new-Štokavian and Ikavian – the Bunjevci Croats form an integral part of the Croatian national corpus."
